title: Governing equations
content_summary: This chapter describes the governing conservation equations used in blastFoam. It covers two main cases: a single-fluid model with a shared velocity and energy, and a multi-fluid model where each fluid has its own velocity and energy. The term 'multi-phase' is used to describe a fluid that utilizes multiple equations of state for the density-energy-pressure relationship. The general form of the conservation equation is presented.
content:
Here the governing conservation equations used within blastFoam will be described. We will describe two cases: (1) the single-fluid case where a single shared velocity and energy is used and (2) the multi-fluid case where each fluid has a unique velocity and energy. The phrase "multi-phase" will be used to denote a fluid that uses multiple equations of states to describe the density-energy-pressure relation. For both variations U is the vector of conservative variables, F are the fluxes corresponding to the respective conservative variables, and S is a vector of source terms,

$$\partial_tU + \nabla \cdot F = S \quad (2.1)$$

The exact form each of these take will depend on whether the single-fluid or multi-fluid is used. Each of these are presented in the following sections.

title: Single-fluid model
content_summary: This section defines the conservative variables, fluxes, and source terms for a single-phase system with multiple species. It also describes the model for multiphase systems where phases share a single velocity and energy, but individual phase masses are conserved. The mixture's properties like density, velocity, and energy are used, and the pressure is determined by a specified equation of state.
content:
For a single phase with multiple species, the conservative variables and fluxes are defined as

$$U = \begin{pmatrix} \rho \\ \rho Y_s \\ \rho \mathbf{u} \\ \rho E \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.2)$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix} \rho \mathbf{u} \\ \rho Y_s \mathbf{u} \\ \rho \mathbf{u} \otimes \mathbf{u} + pI \\ (\rho E + p)\mathbf{u} \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.3)$$

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \dot{R}_s + \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_s \\ \dot{M} + \dot{M}_v \\ \dot{E} + \dot{E}_v \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.4)$$

where Ys is the mass fraction of specie s, and Js is the diffusion flux of species s. For multiple phases

$$U = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n \\ \alpha_1\rho_1 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n\rho_n \\ \rho \mathbf{u} \\ \rho E \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.5)$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 \mathbf{u} \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n \mathbf{u} \\ \alpha_1\rho_1 \mathbf{u} \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n\rho_n \mathbf{u} \\ \rho \mathbf{u} \otimes \mathbf{u} + pI \\ (\rho E + p)\mathbf{u} \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.6)$$

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ \dot{M} + \dot{M}_v \\ \dot{E} + \dot{E}_v \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.7)$$

where ρ is the mixture density, u is the mixture velocity, E is the total energy (thermal and kinetic), p is the pressure, and ρi and αi are the density and volume fraction of each phase. The source terms, Rs, M, and E are the specie production from reactions, momentum, and energy sources (including gravitational acceleration), and Mv and Ev are viscous terms. The sum of all volume fractions is defined to be one, i.e.

$$\sum_i \alpha_i = 1 \quad (2.8)$$

When only two phases are used, the second volume fraction is defined

$$\alpha_2 = 1 - \alpha_1 \quad (2.9)$$

Additionally, the mixture density is defined by

$$\rho = \sum_i \alpha_i\rho_i \quad (2.10)$$

When all phases are considered inviscid, Mv = Ev = 0. However, when viscosity is included,

$$\dot{M}_v = \nabla \cdot \left( \rho\nu \left[ \nabla \mathbf{u} + (\nabla \mathbf{u})^T - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u})I \right] \right) \quad (2.11)$$

and

$$\dot{E}_v = \nabla \cdot \left( \rho\nu \left[ \nabla \mathbf{u} + (\nabla \mathbf{u})^T - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u})I \right] \cdot \mathbf{u} \right) + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_T\nabla e) \quad (2.12)$$

where ν is the viscosity, αT is the thermal diffusivity, and I is the identity matrix. The five equation model is used for multiphase systems in which a single shared velocity, a shared internal energy and temperature is used, and individual phase masses are conserved. Pressure is defined using a specified EOS, where the mixture’s internal energy, densities, and volume fraction are used to calculate the total pressure.

title: Multi-fluid
content_summary: This section details the multi-fluid model, where each phase has its own distinct set of mass, momentum, and energy. Unlike the single-fluid model, conservation of these properties is maintained through interfacial transfer models that account for interactions like drag and heat transfer between phases. The conservation equations are presented for an individual fluid phase, including source terms for momentum and energy exchange, viscous effects, and reactions.
content:
Similar to flows that use a single shared velocity and energy, the mass, momentum, and energy are all conserved. In contrast, however, each phase has its own set of mass, momentum, and energy, which is transferred between phases using interfacial transfer models (such as drag, heat transfer, etc.).

The conservation equations for the i-th fluid phase are

$$U_i = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i E_i \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.13)$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \otimes \mathbf{u}_i + \alpha_i p_i I \\ \alpha_i(\rho_i E_i + p_i)\mathbf{u}_i \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.14)$$

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + \sum_{i\neq j} \frac{\dot{\omega}_{ij}}{I(\rho_i, \rho_j)} \\ \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{\omega}_{ij} \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{g} + \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{M}_{ij} + \dot{M}_{v,i} \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + \sum_{i\neq j} (\dot{E}_{ij} + \dot{M}_{ij} \cdot \mathbf{u}_{int,ij}) + \dot{E}_{det,i} + \dot{E}_{v,i} \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.15)$$

where αi is the phase volume fraction, ρi is the phase density, ui is the phase velocity, Ei is the total phase energy, and pi is the phase pressure. The source terms, Mij and Eij, are the momentum and energy sources that account for interactions between other phases, Mv,i and Ev,i are viscous terms, g is the gravitational acceleration vector, Edet,i is the energy added due to reactions, and uint,ij is the interfacial velocity between two phases. When mass transfer is used ωi,j is the mass transfer rate from the i-th phase to the j-th phase, and I is a switching function used to select the transferring quantity (Xue et al., 2011) and is defined as

$$I(x_i, x_j) = \xi_{i,j}x_j + (1 - \xi_{i,j})x_i \quad (2.16)$$

and

$$\xi_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \dot{\omega}_{i,j} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (2.17)$$

The viscous stress uses a Newtonian stress tensor defined as

$$\dot{M}_{v,i} = \nabla \cdot \left( \alpha_i\rho_i\nu_i \left[ \nabla \mathbf{u}_i + (\nabla \mathbf{u}_i)^T - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i)I \right] \right) \quad (2.18)$$

and

$$\dot{E}_{v,i} = \nabla \cdot \left( \alpha_i\rho_i\nu_i \left[ \nabla \mathbf{u}_i + (\nabla \mathbf{u}_i)^T - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i)I \right] \cdot \mathbf{u}_i \right) + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_i\alpha_{T,i}\nabla e_i) \quad (2.19)$$

where νi is the viscosity, αT,i is the thermal diffusivity, and I is the identity matrix.
The momentum transfer term, Mij, is defined as

$$\dot{M}_{ij} = \dot{M}_{D,ij} + \dot{M}_{L,ij} + \dot{M}_{VM,ij} + \dot{M}_{WL,ij} + \dot{M}_{TD,ij} + \dot{\omega}_{i,j}I(\mathbf{u}_i, \mathbf{u}_j) \quad (2.20)$$

where the summed terms, in order, represent drag, lift, virtual mass, wall lubrication, and turbulent dispersion, respectively.
The energy transfer term, Eij, is defined as

$$\dot{E}_{ij} = \dot{E}_{HT,ij} + \dot{\omega}_{i,j}I(E_i, E_j + h_{fi} - h_{fj}) \quad (2.21)$$

where EHT,ij is the energy transfer rate due to convective heat transfer, and hf is the heat of formation.

title: Granular phases
content_summary: This section describes the governing equations for granular phases. Unlike standard fluids, granular fluids track not only mass, momentum, and thermal energy but also granular energy, which arises from fluctuations in phase velocity within a cell. This granular energy is related to the granular temperature. The equations for conservative variables, fluxes, and source terms are presented, introducing new quantities like granular pressure, frictional pressure, and interfacial pressure. Source terms for momentum, energy, and granular energy dissipation and production are defined. It is noted that in highly compressible flows, the heat generated from inelastic collisions can be significant. The section provides further references for a deeper understanding of granular flows and kinetic theory.
content:
In contrast to a standard fluid (gas or liquid), a granular fluid tracks the phase mass, momentum, thermal energy, and granular energy. The last quantity, granular energy, is the energy that is present due to fluctuation in the phase velocity within a cell. This is the kinetic energy due to the local velocity variance. The more common quantity is the granular temperature, Θi, which is related to the granular energy by E_i = 3/2 * Θi. The governing equations are

$$U_i = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i\rho_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i e_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i E_i \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.22)$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i \otimes \mathbf{u}_i + (P_{s,i} + P_{fric,i})I \\ \alpha_i\rho_i e_i \mathbf{u}_i \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u}_i E_i \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.23)$$

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{\omega}_{ij} \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{g} - \alpha_i\sum_l \nabla p_{int,il} + \sum_j \dot{M}_{ij} + \dot{M}_{v,i} \\ \sum_j \dot{e}_{ij} + \sum_k \dot{\gamma}_{ik} + \dot{e}_{det,i} \\ -\sum_k \dot{\gamma}_{ik} - \sum_l \dot{\phi}_{prod,il} \\ - P_{s,i}\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + \alpha_i\tau_i : \nabla \mathbf{u}_i \\ + \nabla \cdot (\kappa_{s,i}\nabla\Theta_i) + \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{\omega}_{ij}I(E_i, 0) \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.24)$$

where, again, αi is the phase volume fraction, ρi is the phase density, ui is the phase velocity, and ei is the phase thermal energy. The summations Σj, Σk, and Σl denote summations over all phases, all solid phases, and all fluid phases, respectively. The new quantities, Ps,i, Pfric,i, E, and pint are the granular pressure, frictional pressure, granular energy, and interfacial pressure (generally taken as the gas phase pressure), respectively. The density is assumed to be constant, so the volume fraction is found by simply dividing the total phase mass by the known density. The source terms, Mij, eij, γij, and φprod,il are the momentum transfer, energy transfer, granular energy dissipation, and production terms. The momentum and energy transfer terms are the same as for a fluid phase (eij = Eij and edet,i = Edet,i). The production and dissipation terms are described below.

The production and dissipation of granular energy introduce a new term into the continuous phase total energy equation so that the continuous phase source terms become

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + \sum_{i\neq j} \frac{\dot{\omega}_{ij}}{I(\rho_i, \rho_j)} \\ \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{\omega}_{ij} \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{g} + \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{M}_{ij} + \dot{M}_{v,i} \\ \alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{u}_i + \sum_{i\neq j} \dot{E}_{ij} + \dot{E}_{det,i} + \dot{E}_{v,i} + \sum_k \dot{\phi}_{prod,k} \\ \sum_k \dot{\omega}_{ik}I(0, E_k) \end{pmatrix} \quad (2.25)$$

You can also see in Eq. (2.22) that the dissipation of granular energy is converted into thermal energy within the solid phase. For those familiar with incompressible flows, this term is typically neglected due to the fact that the amount of heat produced from collisions is typically very small. In highly compressible flows, this is not the case, and significant heat can be generated due to the loss of energy from inelastic collisions. We define the change in momentum due to granular phase stress as

$$\dot{M}_{ij} = \nabla \cdot \tau_{s,i} + \dot{\omega}_{i,j}I(\mathbf{u}_i, \mathbf{u}_j) \quad (2.26)$$

and the granular stress is defined as

$$\mathbf{\tau}_{s,i} = \rho_i\nu_{s,i}[\nabla \mathbf{u}_i + (\nabla \mathbf{u}_i)^T] + \left(\lambda_{s,i} - \frac{2}{3}\rho_i\nu_{s,i}\right)(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i)I \quad (2.27)$$

with λs,i being the bulk viscosity of the granular phase defined by (Huilin and Gidaspow, 2003)

$$\lambda_{s,i} = \sum_k P_{s,ik} \frac{d_{ik}}{3} \sqrt{\frac{2(m_i\Theta_i + m_k\Theta_k)^2}{\pi\Theta_i\Theta_k(m_i^2\Theta_i + m_k^2\Theta_k)}} \quad (2.28)$$

where Ps,ij is the granular pressure between solid phases i and k, and is related to Ps,i by

$$P_{s,i} = \alpha_i\rho_i\Theta_i + \sum_k P_{s,ik} \quad (2.29)$$

The production of granular energy is given by

$$\phi_{prod,il} = \frac{81\alpha_i\mu_l^2}{g_{0,ii}d_i^3\rho_i\sqrt{\pi}} \frac{|\mathbf{u}_l - \mathbf{u}_i|^2}{\sqrt{\Theta_i}} - 3K_{D,il}\Theta_i \quad (2.30)$$

where KD,il is the drag coefficient for the i-l solid-fluid pair and g0,ij is the radial distribution function. The dissipation of granular energy between granular phases i and k is defined by (Huilin and Gidaspow, 2003)

$$\dot{\gamma}_{ik} = \left[ \frac{3}{d_{ik}} \left( \frac{2m_0^2\Theta_i\Theta_k}{\pi(m_i^2\Theta_i + m_k^2\Theta_k)} \right)^{1/2} - \frac{3m_0(m_i\Theta_i + m_k\Theta_k)}{4(m_i^2\Theta_i + m_k^2\Theta_k)}\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}_i \right] (1 - e_{ik})P_{s,ik} \quad (2.31)$$

where

$$m_i = \frac{\rho_i\pi d_i^3}{6} \quad (2.32)$$

dik = (di + dk)/2, m0 = mi + mk, eik is the coefficient of restitution between granular phases i and j, and Ps,ik is the granular pressure between phases i and k. The drag, radial distribution function, and granular pressure are determined by models and will be discussed in Sec. 3.1 and Sec. 4. The specific manner as to how these source terms are solved can be found in Appendix B.7 of Houim and Oran (2016). The energy transfer term, eij, is defined as

$$\dot{e}_{ij} = \dot{e}_{HT,ij} + \dot{\omega}_{i,j}I(e_i, e_j + h_{fi} - h_{fj}) \quad (2.33)$$

Lastly, the speed of sound for a granular material is given by

$$c_i^2 = \frac{1}{\rho_i} \left[ \frac{\partial P_{tot,i}}{\partial \alpha_i} + \frac{2}{3}\Theta_i \left( \frac{\partial P_{tot,i}}{\partial \Theta_i} \right)^2 \frac{1}{\rho_i\alpha_i^2} \right] \quad (2.34)$$

where Ptot,i = Ps,i + Pfr,i. Because different models can be used for the granular pressure, frictional pressure, and additional models used within radial distribution functions, the specific form this takes is not discussed here. The topic of granular flows, or kinetic theory, is quite extensive and thus it would be impractical to attempt to provide complete descriptions and derivations of the equations and models discussed here. For more more details on this topic, the reader should consult the following resources:

For derivations relating to molecular gasses, see Chapman et al. (1990)
For more practical derivations, see Gidaspow (1994)
For applications to compressible flows, see Fox (2019)
For derivations of the polydisperse formulations, see Huilin and Gidaspow (2003) and Chao et al. (2011)

title: Interfacial models
content_summary: This chapter describes the interfacial models used to determine the exchange of momentum and energy between phases. These models are generally solved using an explicit Euler integration method, except for drag and heat transfer, which are solved analytically. The focus is on models applicable to gas-particle flows, with other models to be added as they become relevant.
content:
The exchange of momentum and energy is determined by the use of interfacial models. These models are described below and include the equations used to calculate the transfer rates. In general, these models are solved using an explicit Euler integration method. Drag and heat transfer, however, are solved using an analytical solution; the reference used for these solution methods will be provided in the relevant sections.

NOTE: There are more models currently implemented that do not apply to gas-particle flows and, therefore, are not presented at this time. Their descriptions and equations will be added as they become relevant.

title: Drag models
content_summary: This section explains that drag models are responsible for relaxing the velocity difference between two phases towards an equilibrium. The rate of this relaxation is determined by a drag coefficient. The momentum transfer term is defined, and it's noted that the solution can be found analytically or through a more computationally expensive ODE solver.
content:
The drag is responsible for relaxing the velocity difference between two phases to an equilibrium velocity at a rate determined by the drag coefficient, KD,ij:

$$K_{D,ij} = 0.75 \frac{\alpha_d C_D Re_{ij} \rho_c \nu_c}{d_d^2} \quad (3.1)$$

where the Reynolds number between phases i and j is defined as Reij = dd|uc−ud|/νc. The subscripts c and d are used to denote the continuous and dispersed phases, respectively. The term CDReij will be defined by the models discussed in the following sections. The momentum transfer term is defined as MD,ij = KD,ij(uj − ui). The solution to the drag relaxation can be found in Appendix B.7 of Houim and Oran (2016). Additionally, an ODE solver can be used to solve the drag relaxation, though this approach is much more computationally expensive.

title: SchillerNaumann
content_summary: This drag model calculates the drag coefficient for flow over a single sphere.
content:
The drag model of Schiller and Naumann (1933) calculates the drag coefficient for flow over a single sphere:

$$C_D Re_{ij} = \begin{cases} 24(1 + 0.15Re_{ij}^{0.687}) & \text{if } Re_{ij} < 1000 \\ 0.44Re_{ij} & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (3.2)$$

title: WenYu
content_summary: This drag model is designed for dispersed particulate flow. The drag coefficient is defined by modifying the Schiller-Naumann drag coefficient, weighting it by the dispersed phase volume fraction.
content:
The Wen and Yu drag model (Enwald et al., 1996) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is defined as

$$C_D Re_{ij} = C_D Re_{SN,ij} \alpha_c^{-2.65} \quad (3.3)$$

where CDReSN,ij is the Schiller Naumann drag coefficient multiplied by the Reynolds number, and the Reynolds number used in the drag coefficient is weighted by the dispersed phase volume fraction, i.e.,

$$Re_{s,ij} = Re_{ij} \alpha_c \quad (3.4)$$

title: Ergun
content_summary: This drag model is used for dispersed particulate flow, with a specific formula for the drag coefficient.
content:
The Ergun drag model (Enwald et al., 1996) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is defined as

$$C_D Re_{ij} = \frac{4}{3} \frac{150(1 - \alpha_c)}{\alpha_c} + 1.75Re_{ij} \quad (3.5)$$

title: Gibilaro
content_summary: This drag model is used for dispersed particulate flow, with the drag coefficient defined by a specific formula.
content:
The Gibilaro drag model (Enwald et al., 1996) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is defined as

$$C_D Re_{ij} = \frac{4}{3} \left( \frac{17.3}{\alpha_2} + 0.336Re_{ij} \right) \alpha_c \alpha_2^{-2.8} \quad (3.6)$$

where α2 = 1 − αd.

title: SyamlalOBrien
content_summary: This drag model is intended for dispersed particulate flow and defines the drag coefficient through a set of related equations.
content:
The Syamlal O’Brien drag model (Syamlal et al., 1993) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is defined as

$$C_D Re_{ij} = \frac{C_{D,s}Re_{ij}\alpha_c}{V_r^2} \quad (3.7)$$

where

$$C_{D,s}Re_{ij} = (0.63\sqrt{Re_{ij}} + 4.8\sqrt{V_r})^2 \quad (3.8)$$

$$V_r = 0.5\left(A - 0.06Re_{ij} + \sqrt{(0.06Re_{ij})^2 + 0.12Re_{ij}(2B - A) + A^2}\right) \quad (3.9)$$

$$A = \alpha_2^{4.14} \quad (3.10)$$

$$B = \begin{cases} 0.8\alpha_2^{1.28} & \text{if } \alpha_2 < 0.85 \\ \alpha_2^{2.65} & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (3.11)$$

and α2 = 1 − αd.

title: GidaspowErgunWenYu
content_summary: This drag model is for dispersed particulate flow and combines the Wen and Yu drag model for dilute conditions with the Ergun drag model for dense conditions.
content:
The drag model of Gidaspow (1994) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is a combination of the Wen and Yu drag model (dilute) and Ergun drag model (dense):

$$C_D Re_{ij} = \begin{cases} C_D Re_{WenYu,ij} & \text{if } \alpha_c > 0.80 \\ C_D Re_{Ergun,ij} & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (3.12)$$

where CDReWenYu,ij is the Wen Yu drag coefficient and CDReErgun,ij is the Ergun drag coefficient, both multiplied by the Reynolds number.

title: GidaspowSchillerNaumann
content_summary: This drag model is for dispersed particulate flow. The drag coefficient is defined by modifying a base coefficient, with the Reynolds number used in the calculation being weighted by the dispersed phase volume fraction.
content:
The Gidaspow-Schiller Naumann drag model (Enwald et al., 1996) is used for dispersed particulate flow and the drag coefficient is defined as

$$C_D Re_{ij} = C_{D,s}Re_{ij}\alpha_c^{-1.65} \quad (3.13)$$

$$C_{D,s}Re_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{24(1+0.15Re_{s,ij}^{0.687})}{\alpha_c} & \text{if } Re_{s,ij} < 1000 \\ 0.44Re_{s,ij} & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (3.14)$$

where the Reynolds number used in the drag coefficient is weighted by the dispersed phase volume fraction, i.e.,

$$Re_{s,ij} = Re_{ij}\alpha_c \quad (3.15)$$

title: Lift models
content_summary: This section describes the lift interfacial momentum transfer model, which accounts for acceleration due to rotation in the continuous phase. The model's definition is provided.
content:
The lift interfacial momentum transfer model is used to account for acceleration due to rotation in the continuous phase and is defined as

$$\dot{M}_{L,ij} = C_{L,ij}\alpha_d\rho_c [(\mathbf{u}_c - \mathbf{u}_i) \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{u}_c)] \quad (3.16)$$

where CL,ij is the lift coefficient between phases i and j and is defined by the model of choice.

title: constant
content_summary: This model assumes the lift coefficient is a constant value.
content: The lift coefficient is a constant value.

title: Heat transfer models
content_summary: This section explains that the heat transfer model is responsible for relaxing the temperature difference between two phases, with the rate determined by the chosen model. The Prandtl number is defined, and it is noted that the solution to temperature relaxation can be found in an appendix.
content:
The heat transfer model is responsible for relaxing the temperature between two phases at a rate determined by the model of choice. The Prandtl number will be used and is defined as

$$Pr_{ij} = \frac{\nu_c C_{vc} \rho_c}{\kappa_c} \quad (3.17)$$

Again, the subscripts c and d represent the continuous and dispersed phases, respectively. Cvc is the continuous phase specific heat at constant volume and κc is the continuous phase thermal conductivity. The solution to the temperature relaxation can be found in Appendix B.7 of Houim and Oran (2016).

title: RanzMarshall
content_summary: This heat transfer model defines the heat transfer rate using a formula where the Nusselt number is a run-time selectable model, and other properties are taken from the thermodynamic model.
content:
The Ranz Marshall heat transfer model (Houim and Oran, 2016) defines the heat transfer rate as

$$\dot{E}_{HT,ij} = \frac{6\alpha_d\kappa_c Nu_{ij}}{d_d^2} \quad (3.18)$$

where the Nusselt number is a run-time selectable model, and all other properties are taken from the thermodynamic model.

table.Description: Stabilization value of volume fraction (defaults to that of the phaseModel)

title: noneNu
content_summary: This option should only be used if a Nusselt number is required by another model, but heat transfer itself is neglected.
content: This should only be used is a Nusselt number is needed by some other model, but heat transfer is neglected.

title: Nusselt number models
content_summary: This section is a placeholder for various Nusselt number models.

title: constant
content_summary: In this heat transfer model, the Nusselt number is a constant value provided by the user.
content: For the constant Nusselt number heat transfer model, Eq. (3.18) is again used, but the Nusselt number is a constant value provided by the user.

title: duct
content_summary: For hollow particles, this model provides the Nusselt number as defined by Dittus and Boelter (1930).
content:
For hollow particles the Nusselt number of Dittus and Boelter (1930) is defined as

$$Nu_{ij} = 0.023Re_{ij}^{0.8}Pr_{ij}^{0.4} \quad (3.19)$$

title: GelperinEinstein
content_summary: This model defines the Nusselt number according to Gelperin and Einstein (1971).
content:
The Nusselt number of Gelperin and Einstein (1971) is defined as

$$Nu_{ij} = 0.023Re_{ij}^{2/3}Pr_{ij}^{1/3} \quad (3.20)$$

title: Gunn
content_summary: This model defines the Nusselt number according to Gunn (1978).
content:
The Nusselt number of Gunn (1978) is defined as

$$Nu_{ij} = (7 - 10\alpha_c + 5.0\alpha_c^2)(1 + 0.7Re_{ij}^{0.2}Pr_{ij}^{1/3}) + (1.33 - 2.4\alpha_c + 1.2\alpha_c^2)Re_{ij}^{0.7}Pr_{ij}^{1/3} \quad (3.21)$$

title: RanzMarschall
content_summary: This model provides the Ranz-Marschall Nusselt number definition for spheres.
content:
The Ranz-Marschall Nusselt number for spheres is defined as

$$Nu_{ij} = 2 + 0.6Re_{ij}^{1/2}Pr_{ij}^{1/3} \quad (3.22)$$

title: sphere
content_summary: This model defines the Nusselt number for spheres.
content:
The Nusselt number for spheres is defined as

$$Nu_{ij} = 2 + 0.4Re_{ij}^{1/2}Pr_{ij}^{1/3} \quad (3.23)$$

title: Mass transfer models
content_summary: This section notes that currently, only particle-to-fluid mass transfer has been implemented; fluid-fluid mass transfer is not supported.
content: Currently on particle to fluid mass transfer has been implemented. Fluid-fluid mass transfer is not currently supported.

title: none
content_summary: This option signifies that no mass transfer occurs.
content: No mass transfer.

title: reactingParticle
content_summary: In this model, the mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated from the change in a particle's mass. The regression rate is determined by a reaction rate, requiring the use of the reactingParticle diameter model.
content: The mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated using the change in mass of a particle where the regression rate is determined by a reaction rate. the reactingParticle diameter model must be used.

title: Interfacial pressure models
content_summary: This section explains that the interfacial pressure model is used to determine the pressure at the interface between two phases.
content: The interfacial pressure model is used to determine the pressure at the interface between two phases.

title: single
content_summary: This model uses the pressure of a single, specified phase. It is recommended for gas-particle flows, using the gas phase pressure.
content:
The pressure of the given phase is used.

NOTE: For gas-particle flows this option should be used, using the gas phase pressure.

title: volumeAveraged
content_summary: This model uses the volume-fraction-averaged pressure of the two phases.
content: The volume fraction averaged pressure of the two phases is used.

title: totalPressure
content_summary: This model uses the volume-fraction-averaged thermodynamic pressure and the mass-averaged dynamic pressure.
content: The volume fraction averaged thermodynamic pressure and the mass averaged dynamic pressure is used.

title: Interfacial velocity models
content_summary: This section explains that the interfacial velocity model is used to determine the velocity at the interface between two phases.
content: The interfacial velocity model is used to determine the velocity at the interface between two phases.

title: single
content_summary: This model uses the velocity of a single, specified phase. It is recommended for gas-particle flows, using the particle phase velocity.
content:
The velocity of the given phase is used.

NOTE: For gas-particle flows this option should be used, using the particle phase velocity.

title: massAveraged
content_summary: This model uses the mass-averaged velocity of the two phases.
content: The mass averaged velocity of the two phases is used.

title: Granular models
content_summary: This chapter details additional models required for granular phases to account for particle-particle collisions in both kinetic and frictional regimes. These include models for radial distribution, viscosity, conductivity, granular pressure, frictional pressure, solid-solid drag, and packing. Common variables used in these models, such as strain and total granular drag, are defined.
content:
For granular phases, additional models are required to account for particle-particle collisions in both the kinetic and frictional regimes. These models include radial distribution, viscosity, conductivity, granular pressure, frictional pressure, solid-solid drag, and packing models. Some common variables used in the granular models will now be defined. Here, Σk is the summation over all granular phase indices and αs is the sum of all granular phase volume fractions. The strain is defined as

$$S = \frac{1}{2}[(\nabla u)^T + \nabla u] - \frac{1}{3}(\nabla \cdot u)I \quad (4.1)$$

The symmetric strain is defined as

$$D = \frac{1}{2}[(\nabla u)^T + \nabla u] \quad (4.2)$$

Finally, the total granular drag on a phase is defined as

$$K_{D,i} = \sum_k K_{D,ik} \quad (4.3)$$

title: Radial distribution models
content_summary: This section explains that the radial distribution model gives the likelihood of a collision between two particles, defined for both like and unlike particle phases.
content: The radial distribution gives the likelihood of a collision between two particles. This is defined between like and unlike particle phases.

title: CarnahanStarling
content_summary: The Carnahan-Starling radial distribution function is valid only for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Carnahan Starling radial distribution function is only valid for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$g_{0,ii} = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha_i} + \frac{3\alpha_i}{2(1 - \alpha_i)^2} + \frac{\alpha_i^2}{2(1 - \alpha_i)^3} \quad (4.4)$$

title: SinclairJackson
content_summary: The Sinclair and Jackson (1989) radial distribution function is valid only for monodisperse flows.
content:
The radial distribution of Sinclair and Jackson (1989) function is only valid for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$g_{0,ii} = \frac{1}{1 - (\frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_{i,max}})^{1/3}} \quad (4.5)$$

title: LunSavage
content_summary: The Lun et al. (1984) radial distribution function is valid only for monodisperse flows.
content:
The radial distribution function of Lun et al. (1984) is only valid for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$g_{0,ii} = \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_{max,i}}\right)^{-2.5\alpha_{max,i}} \quad (4.6)$$

title: Gao
content_summary: The Gao et al. (2008) radial distribution function is a modified version of the Sinclair-Jackson function and is valid for polydisperse flows.
content:
The radial distribution function of Gao et al. (2008) is a modified version of the Sinclair Jackson radial distribution function and is valid for polydisperse flows. It is defined as

$$g_{0,ij} = \frac{d_i g_{0,i} + d_j g_{0,j}}{d_i + d_j} \quad (4.7)$$

$$g_{0,i} = \frac{1}{1 - (\frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_{i,max}})^{1/3}} \quad (4.8)$$

title: Lebowitz
content_summary: The Lebowitz radial distribution function (Benyahia et al., 2012) is valid for polydisperse flows.
content:
The Lebowitz radial distribution function (Benyahia et al., 2012) is valid for polydisperse flows and is defined as

$$g_{0,ij} = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha_s} + \frac{3d_i d_j}{(1 - \alpha_s)^2(d_i + d_j)} \sum_k \frac{\alpha_k}{d_k} \quad (4.9)$$

title: Granular viscosity models
content_summary: This section explains that granular viscosity modeling is used to determine the stress in a granular phase. These models only account for single collisions; frictional models will contribute additional stress for multiple collisions.
content: The modeling of granular viscosity is used to determine the stress seen in a granular phase. These models only account for single collisions (frictional models will have additional contributions for multiple collisions).

title: Gidaspow
content_summary: The Gidaspow viscosity model (Gidaspow, 1994) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Gidaspow viscosity model (Gidaspow, 1994) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\nu_{s,i} = \frac{4}{5}\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{i,j})}{\pi} + \frac{1}{15}\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}(1 + e_{ii})\alpha_i^2 + \frac{1}{6}\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i + \frac{10}{96}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{(1 + e_{ii})g_{0,ii}} \quad (4.10)$$

title: HrenyaSinclair
content_summary: The Hrenya-Sinclair viscosity model (Hrenya and Sinclair, 1997) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Hrenya Sinclair viscosity model (Hrenya and Sinclair, 1997) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\nu_{s,i} = \frac{4}{5}\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{i,j})}{\pi} + \frac{1}{15}\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}(3e_{ii} - 1) \frac{\alpha_i^2}{3 - e_{ii}} + \frac{1}{6}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i(0.5\lambda + 0.25(3e_{ii} - 1))}{0.5(3 - e_{ii})\lambda} + \frac{10}{96}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{(1 + e_{ii})0.5(3 - e_{ii})g_{0,ii}\lambda} \quad (4.11)$$

where

$$\lambda = 1 + \frac{d_i L}{6\sqrt{2}\alpha_i} \quad (4.12)$$

title: Princeton
content_summary: The Princeton viscosity model (Agrawal et al., 2001) is for polydisperse flows.
content:
The Princeton viscosity model (Agrawal et al., 2001) is for polydisperse flows and is defined as

$$\nu_{s,i} = \rho_i \frac{3.6}{3} \left[ \frac{\mu_i^*}{g_{0,ii}\eta(2 - \eta)} \left(1 + \frac{8}{5}\eta \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik})\right) \times \left(1 + \frac{8}{5}\eta(3\eta - 2) \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0i,k})\right) + \frac{3}{5}\eta\mu_b \right] \quad (4.13)$$

where

$$\mu_i^* = \frac{\rho_i\alpha_i g_{0,ii} \Theta_i \mu}{\rho_i \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \Theta_i + \frac{2K_{d,i}\mu}{\rho_i\alpha_i}} \quad (4.14)$$

$$\mu = \frac{5}{96}\rho_i d_i \sqrt{\pi\Theta_i} \quad (4.15)$$

and

$$\mu_b = \frac{256}{5\pi} \mu\alpha_i \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \quad (4.16)$$

title: Syamlal
content_summary: The Syamlal viscosity model (Syamlal et al., 1993) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Syamlal viscosity model (Syamlal et al., 1993) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\nu_{s,i} = \frac{4}{5}\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{ii})}{\pi} + \frac{1}{15}\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}(3e_{ii} - 1) \frac{\alpha_i^2}{3 - e_{ii}} + \frac{1}{6}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i}{(3 - e_{ii})} \quad (4.17)$$

title: none
content_summary: This option signifies that no granular viscosity is used, which also removes the conductivity contribution to the granular energy equation.
content: No granular viscosity is used. This also removes the conductivity contribution to the granular energy equation.

title: Granular conductivity models
content_summary: This section introduces models for granular conductivity.

title: Chao
content_summary: The Chao granular conductivity model (Chao et al., 2011) is defined by a specific set of equations.
content:
The Chao granular conductivity model Chao et al. (2011) is defined as

$$\kappa_{s,i} = \frac{2\kappa_{dilute,i}}{\frac{1}{N} \sum_k (1 + e_{ik})g_{0,ik}} \left[1 + \frac{6}{5} \sum_k \alpha_k(1 + e_{ik})g_{0,ik}\right]^2 \quad (4.18)$$

where N is the number of granular phases and

$$\kappa_{dilute,i} = \frac{15\rho_i\Theta_i}{\sum_k \pi\sqrt{2\pi}n_k d_{ij}^2(\sqrt{\Theta_i} + \sqrt{\Theta_j} - 0.56\sqrt[4]{\Theta_i\Theta_j})} \quad (4.19)$$

title: Gidaspow
content_summary: The Gidaspow conductivity model (Gidaspow, 1994) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Gidaspow conductivity model (Gidaspow, 1994) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\kappa_{s,i} = 2\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{ii})}{\sqrt{\pi}} + \frac{9}{8}\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}0.5(1 + e_{ii})\alpha_i^2 + \frac{15}{16}\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i + \frac{25}{64}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{g_{0,ii}(1 + e_{ii})} \quad (4.20)$$

title: HrenyaSinclair
content_summary: The Hrenya-Sinclair conductivity model (Hrenya and Sinclair, 1997) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Hrenya Sinclair conductivity model (Hrenya and Sinclair, 1997) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\kappa_{s,i} = 2\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{ii})}{\sqrt{\pi}} + \frac{9}{8}\frac{\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}0.25(1 + e_{ii})^2(2e_{ii} - 1)\alpha_i^2}{\frac{49}{16} - \frac{33}{16}e_{ii}} + \frac{15}{16}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i(0.5e_{ii}^2 + 0.25e_{ii} - 0.75 + \lambda)}{(\frac{49}{16} - \frac{33}{16}e_{ii})\lambda} + \frac{25}{64}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{g_{0,ii}\lambda(1 + e_{ii})(\frac{49}{16} - \frac{33}{16}e_{ii})} \quad (4.21)$$

where

$$\lambda = 1 + \frac{d_i L}{6\sqrt{2}\alpha_i} \quad (4.22)$$

title: Princeton
content_summary: The Princeton conductivity model (Agrawal et al., 2001) is for polydisperse flows.
content:
The Princeton conductivity model (Agrawal et al., 2001) is for polydisperse flows and is defined as

$$\kappa_{s,i} = \frac{\kappa_i^*}{g_{0,ii}} \left[ 1 + \frac{12}{5}\eta \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \right] \left[ 1 + \frac{12}{5}\eta^2(4\eta - 3) \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \right] + \frac{64}{25\pi}(41 - 33\eta)\eta^2 \left[ \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \right]^2 \quad (4.23)$$

where

$$\kappa_i^* = \frac{\rho_i\alpha_i g_{0,ii} \Theta_i \kappa_i}{\rho_i \sum_k (\alpha_k g_{0,ik}) \Theta_i + \frac{6K_{d,i}\kappa_i}{5\rho_i\alpha_i}} \quad (4.24)$$

and

$$\kappa_i = \frac{75\rho_i d_i \sqrt{\pi\Theta_i}}{48\eta(41 - 33\eta)} \quad (4.25)$$

title: Syamlal
content_summary: The Syamlal conductivity model (Syamlal et al., 1993) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Syamlal conductivity model (Syamlal et al., 1993) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$\kappa_{s,i} = 2\alpha_i^2 g_{0,ii} \frac{(1 + e_{ii})}{\sqrt{\pi}} + \frac{9}{8}\frac{\sqrt{\pi g_{0,ii}}0.25(1 + e_{ii})(2e_{ii} - 1)\alpha_i^2}{\frac{49}{16} - \frac{33}{16}e_{ii}} + \frac{15}{16}\frac{\sqrt{\pi}\alpha_i}{\frac{49}{16} - \frac{33}{16}e_{ii}} \quad (4.26)$$

title: Granular pressure models
content_summary: This section explains that the granular pressure model is used to calculate the pressure due to single collisions between particles. Pressures from multiple collisions are handled by frictional models.
content: The granular pressure model is used to calculate the pressure due to single collisions between particles. Pressures that occur due to multiple collisions will be added using the frictional models.

title: Huilin
content_summary: The Huilin granular pressure model (Huilin and Gidaspow, 2003) is for polydisperse flows.
content:
The Huilin granular pressure model (Huilin and Gidaspow, 2003) is for polydisperse flows and is defined as

$$P_{s,ij} = \frac{\pi(1 + e_{ij})d_{ij}^3 g_{0,ij} n_i n_j m_i m_j m_0 \Theta_i \Theta_j}{3(m_i^2\Theta_i + m_j^2\Theta_j)} \times \left[ \frac{m_0^2\Theta_i\Theta_j}{(m_i^2\Theta_i + m_j^2\Theta_j)(\Theta_i + \Theta_j)} \right]^{3/2} \times (1 - 3\omega + 6\omega^2 - 10\omega^3) \quad (4.27)$$

with

$$\omega = \frac{m_i\Theta_i - m_j\Theta_j}{[(m_i^2\Theta_i^2 + m_j^2\Theta_j^2) + \Theta_i\Theta_j(m_i^2 + m_j^2)]^{1/2}} \quad (4.28)$$

title: Lun
content_summary: The Lun granular pressure model (Lun et al., 1984) is for monodisperse flows.
content:
The Lun granular pressure model (Lun et al., 1984) is for monodisperse flows and is defined as

$$P_{s,ij} = \alpha_i \alpha_j \rho_i \Theta_i 2(1 + e_{ij})g_{0,ij} \quad (4.29)$$

title: Frictional stress models
content_summary: This section explains that the frictional stress model accounts for non-singular collisions between particles, resulting in very large stress as granular phases approach their packing limit. This modeled pressure and viscosity limits the movement of particles between computational cells and depends on the total volume fraction of particles.
content: The frictional stress model acts as a simple way to account for non-singular collisions between particles and results in very large stress when the granular phases approach their packing limit. This results in both a modeled pressure and viscosity to calculate a stress, and limits the ability of particles to move between computational cells. The frictional pressure and viscosity denoted Pfr and νfr act on all granular phases since the packing of particles is dependent on the total volume fraction of particles.

title: JohnsonJackson
content_summary: The Johnson-Jackson frictional stress model (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b) uses a smooth function to calculate pressure and defines frictional viscosity based on several model coefficients and packing limits.
content:
The Johnson-Jackson frictional stress model (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b) uses a smooth function to calculate the pressure

$$P_{fr} = \begin{cases} Fr \frac{(\alpha_s - \alpha_{crit,s})^{\eta}}{(\alpha_{max,s} - \alpha_s)^p} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.30)$$

and the frictional viscosity is

$$\nu_{fr} = 0.5P_{fr}\sin(\phi) \quad (4.31)$$

where φ is the angle of internal friction, and Fr, η, and p are model coefficients generally taking values of 0.05, 2, and 5 respectively. αcrit,s is the granular particle fraction that the frictional pressure 'turns on', and αmax,s is the maximum packing limit of all granular phases. Generally αcrit,s is taken to be around 0.5.

title: Schaeffer
content_summary: The Schaeffer frictional stress model (Syamlal et al., 1993) uses a power law to calculate frictional viscosity. The pressure is defined based on the ratio of local to critical volume fraction, which is useful for polydisperse systems where the local packing limit can vary.
content:
The Scheffer frictional stress model (Syamlal et al., 1993) uses a power law and the local stress is used to calculate the frictional viscosity. The critical volume fraction, αcrit,s should be a value close to that of the packing limit. Because the local packing limit can vary in space when polydispersity is present, the ratio of the local volume fraction by the critical volume fraction is used, i.e. αcrit,s = (αcrit,s/αmax,s)crit * αmax,s where (αcrit,s/αmax,s)crit is specified by the user and αmax,s is the calculated local packing limit. The pressure is defined as

$$P_{fr} = \begin{cases} 10^{24}(\alpha_s - \alpha_{crit,s})^{10} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.32)$$

and the viscosity is

$$\nu_{fr} = \begin{cases} 0.5 \frac{P_{fr}\sin(\phi)}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}(I_1(D)^2 - I_2(D))}} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.33)$$

where I1(D) is the first in variant (or trace) and I2(D) is the second invariant.

table.Description: Volume fraction ratio friction is “turned on” [ ]

title: JohnsonJacksonSchaeffer
content_summary: The Johnson-Jackson-Schaeffer frictional stress model (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b) uses the frictional pressure from the Johnson-Jackson model and the viscosity from the Schaeffer model.
content:
For the Johnson Jackson-Scheffer frictional stress model (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b), the frictional pressure of Johnson Jackson is used while the viscosity of the Schaeffer model is used.

$$P_{fr} = \begin{cases} Fr \frac{(\alpha_s - \alpha_{crit,s})^{\eta}}{(\alpha_{max,s} - \alpha_s)^p} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.34)$$

$$\nu_{fr} = \begin{cases} 0.5 \frac{P_{fr}\sin(\phi)}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}(I_1(D)^2 - I_2(D))}} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.35)$$

title: Princeton
content_summary: The Princeton model (Agrawal et al., 2001) blends the Johnson-Jackson model with the Schaeffer model based on the local volume fraction, with the critical ratio of volume fractions used as an input.
content:
The Princeton model Agrawal et al. (2001) uses a blending of the Johnson Jackson model when the local volume fraction is greater than αcrit,JJ,s but less than αcrit,Sc,s. As with the Schaeffer model, the critical ratio of (αcrit,Sc,s/αmax,s)crit is used as an input rather than αcrit,Sc,s.

$$P_{fr} = \begin{cases} 10^{24}(\alpha_s - \alpha_{crit,Sc,s})^{10} & \text{if } \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,Sc,s} \\ Fr \frac{(\alpha_s - \alpha_{crit,JJ,s})^{\eta}}{(\alpha_{max,s} - \alpha_s)^p} & \text{if } \alpha_{crit,Sc,s} \le \alpha_s > \alpha_{crit,JJ,s} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.36)$$

The frictional viscosity is defined using

$$\frac{P_{c,k}}{P_{fr}} = \left(1 - \frac{\nabla \cdot u_s}{n\sqrt{2}\sin(\phi)\sqrt{S:S + \Theta_k/d_k^2}}\right)^{n-1} \quad (4.37)$$

$$\nu_{fr,k} = \frac{\sqrt{2}P_{c,k}\sin(\phi)}{\sqrt{S:S + \Theta_k/d_k^2}} \left[ n - (n-1)\left(\frac{P_{c,k}}{P_{fr}}\right)^{\frac{1}{1-n}} \right] \frac{\alpha_k}{\alpha_s} \quad (4.38)$$

and

$$n = \begin{cases} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sin(\phi)} & \text{if } \nabla \cdot u_s \ge 0 \\ 1.03 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.39)$$

table.Description: Volume fraction ratio friction is “turned on” [ ]

title: Packing limit models
content_summary: This section explains the importance of modeling the packing limit in polydisperse flows. When there are large variations in particle diameters, smaller particles can fit between larger ones, increasing the maximum allowable volume fraction. For monodisperse flows, a constant packing limit is sufficient.
content: The modeling of the packing limit is important in polydisperse flows due to the fact that, when large variations in particle diameters exist locally, the packing limit can vary widely. This is due to the fact that smaller particles can be found in between larger particles, increasing the maximum allowable volume fraction. For monodisperse flows, this is not the case and a constant value packing limit should be used.

title: constant
content_summary: This model uses a constant value for the packing limit.
content: A constant value for the packing limit is used.

title: YuStandish
content_summary: The Yu-Standish packing model (Yu and Standish, 1987) assumes that particle phases are ordered from smallest to largest diameter to calculate the packing limit.
content:
The Yu Standish packing model (Yu and Standish, 1987) assumes that the particle phases are ordered from smallest diameter to largest for the calculation of the packing limit. The packing limit is defined as

$$\alpha_{max,s} = \min_{i \in 1:N} \left[ \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} (1 - \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{p_{ik}}) \frac{cx_i}{X_{ik}} - \sum_{k=i+1}^{N} (1 - \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{p_{ik}}) \frac{cx_i}{X_{ik}}} \right] \quad (4.40)$$

where

$$cx_i = \frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_s} \quad (4.41)$$

$$X_{ik} = \begin{cases} \frac{1-r_{ik}^2}{2-\alpha_{max,i}} & \text{if } k < i \\ 1 - \frac{1-r_{ik}^2}{2-\alpha_{max,i}} & \text{if } k \ge i \end{cases} \quad (4.42)$$

$$p_{ik} = \begin{cases} \alpha_{max,i} + \alpha_{max,i}(1 - \alpha_{max,i}) \times (1 - 2.35r_{ik} + 1.35r_{ik}^2) & \text{if } r_{ik} \le 0.741 \\ \alpha_{max,i} & \text{if } r_{ik} > 0.741 \end{cases} \quad (4.43)$$

and

$$r_{ik} = \begin{cases} \frac{d_i}{d_k} & \text{if } i \ge k \\ \frac{d_k}{d_i} & \text{if } i < k \end{cases} \quad (4.44)$$

table.Description: Minimum volume fraction to divide by [ ]

title: FedorsLandel
content_summary: The Fedors-Landel packing model (Fedors and Landel, 1979) is only valid for a binary mixture.
content:
The Fedors-Landel packing model (Fedors and Landel, 1979) is only valid of for a binary mixture and is defined as

$$\alpha_{max,s} = \begin{cases} [(\alpha_{max,1} - \alpha_{max,2}) + (1 - \sqrt{r_{21}}(1 - \alpha_{max,1})\alpha_{max,2})] \times [\alpha_{max,1} + (1 - \alpha_{max,1})\alpha_{max,2}] \frac{cx_i}{\alpha_{max,1}} + \alpha_{max,2} & \text{if } cx_1 \le \frac{\alpha_{max,1}}{\alpha_{max,1}+(1-\alpha_{max,1})\alpha_{max,2}} \\ (1 - \sqrt{r_{21}})[\alpha_{max,1} + (1 - \alpha_{max,1})\alpha_{max,2}]cx_2 + \alpha_{max,1} & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (4.45)$$

Where the definitions of r21 and cxi are used from the previous section.

table.Description: Minimum volume fraction to divide by [ ]

title: Granular drag models
content_summary: This section explains that the drag force between granular phases differs from standard drag models because collisions, which are a function of granular energy, significantly affect the rate at which velocities relax. These models are only necessary when multiple granular phases are used.
content: The drag force between granular phases is different than standard drags models due to the fact the the collisions which are a function of the granular energy strongly effect the rate at which the velocities relax. These granular drag models are only necessary when multiple granular phases are used.

title: Chao
content_summary: The Chao granular drag model (Chao et al., 2011) defines the drag coefficient between phases.
content:
The Chao granular drag model (Chao et al., 2011) defines the drag coefficient between phases as

$$K_{D,ij} = \frac{\alpha_i\alpha_j\rho_i\rho_j}{m_0} d_{ij}(1 + e_{ij})g_{0,ij} \times [\sqrt{2\pi(\sqrt{\Theta_i} + \sqrt{\Theta_j})} - \sqrt{2}(\Theta_i\Theta_j)^{1/4} + 0.5\pi|\mathbf{u}_i - \mathbf{u}_j| - 1.135\frac{|\mathbf{u}_i - \mathbf{u}_j|}{\sqrt{4\Theta_i} + \sqrt{4\Theta_j} - 0.8\sqrt{8\Theta_i\Theta_j}}] \quad (4.46)$$

title: Syamlal
content_summary: The Syamlal granular drag model (Benyahia et al., 2012) defines the drag coefficient between phases based on coefficients of friction.
content:
The Syamlal granular drag model (Benyahia et al., 2012) defines the drag coefficient between phases as

$$K_{D,ij} = \frac{3(1 + e_{ij})(\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{C_{f,ij}\pi^2}{8})\alpha_i\alpha_j\rho_i\rho_j(d_i + d_j)^2 g_{0,ij}|\mathbf{u}_i - \mathbf{u}_j|}{2\pi(\rho_i d_i^2 + \rho_j d_j^3)} + C_1 P_{fr} \quad (4.47)$$

where Cf,ij is the coefficient of friction between granular phases i and j, and C1 is the coefficient of frictional pressure.

title: Fluid and solid thermodynamic models
content_summary: This chapter describes the two types of basic thermodynamic models used in blastFoam solvers: one for fluids and one for solids. Fluid models use internal energy or temperature and density to find pressure and speed of sound. Solid models, used in multi-fluid and multi-region solvers, use a constant density equation of state and model heat conduction. Both model types share basic thermodynamic classes and types (basic, detonating, or multicomponent). To calculate pressure and temperature from conservative quantities, three components are needed: an equation of state, a thermodynamic model, and a transport model, all defined in the phaseProperties file.
content: Within blastFoam solvers, two different types of basic thermodynamic models are used. The primary is that of a fluid where internal energy and density, or temperature and density, are used to find the resulting pressure and speed of sound. The other, is the solid thermodynamic model, which is only used for solid phases within multi-fluid solvers and multi-region solvers. These two models will share the basic thermodynamic classes (eConst, hConst, etc), as well as the choice in the type (basic, detonating, or multicomponent), however the solid phase only can use a constant density equation of state, and the transport model describes the of heat conduction, rather than flow coefficients (like viscosity). These specific choices will be described below. To calculate the pressure and temperature given the conservative quantities (mass, momentum, and energy) three components are required: an equation of state, a thermodynamic model, and a transport model. These models are defined in the phaseProperties file located in the constant directory of each blastFoam case. Each phase within the phaseProperties file has several dictionary entries, and these models are specified in the ”thermoType” sub-dictionary; the molecular weight must also be defined in the ”specie” sub-dictionary.

title: Thermodynamic models
content_summary: This section explains that the thermodynamic model calculates internal energy based on a thermal contribution and an additional contribution from the equation of state. Since internal energy is the tracked quantity, temperature is found using a Newton-Raphson root-finding method. The model can use either internal energy or enthalpy-based coefficients, but total energy is defined only by internal energy. The conversion from enthalpy to internal energy is done using the specified equation of state. By default, internal energy is limited to ensure temperature remains positive, but this can be disabled.
content:
The thermodynamic model is used to calculate the internal energy based solely on the thermal contribution:

$$e_T = \int C_v(T)dT \quad (5.1)$$

An additional contribution from the equation of state is given by:

$$e_{eos}(\rho, T) = \int \left( T\frac{\partial p(\rho, T)}{\partial T} - p(\rho, T) \right) dv \quad (5.2)$$

where vi = 1/ρi is the specific volume. The total internal energy is

$$e = e_T(T) + e_{eos}(\rho, T) \quad (5.3)$$

Because the internal energy is the tracked quantity, the temperature is solved using a Newton-Raphson root finding method given the local internal energy and density. Either internal energy or enthalpy-based coefficients can be used, but only internal energy is used to define the total energy. The conversion from an enthalpy base to an internal energy base is calculated using the specified equation of state. All transport models, with the exception of the tabulated methods were ported from OpenCFD Ltd. (2018b).

$$C_v = C_p - C_pMC_v \quad (5.4)$$

where CpMCv is determined from the equation of state using

$$C_pMC_v = \frac{\partial(p(\rho, e)v)}{\partial T} \quad (5.5)$$

For an ideal gas, CpMCv = R/W where R is the ideal gas constant (8314 [J/mol/K]) and W is the molecular weight in [kg/kmol]. Equations of state that have a pressure with a non-linear dependence on temperature will deviate from this. When enthalpy is used, the internal energy is calculated using

$$e = h - \frac{p}{\rho} \quad (5.6)$$

NOTE: By default the internal energy is limited so that the temperature is greater than 0. If ”limitT” is set to ”no” the temperature and energy will not be limited. Caution should be used with this option.

title: eConst
content_summary: This model calculates internal energy using a constant value for the specific heat at constant volume.
content:
Internal energy is calculated using a constant value of specific heat at constant volume.

$$e_T(T) = C_v(T - T_{ref}) + E_{s,ref} \quad (5.7)$$

table.Description: Reference sensible internal energy [J/kg/] (Tref Cv default)

title: ePolynomial
content_summary: This model calculates internal energy using a polynomial with powers of temperature.
content:
Internal energy is calculated using polynomial with powers of T.

$$e_T(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} C_{v,i}T^i \quad (5.8)$$

table.Description: Specific heat at constant volume polynomial coefficients

title: ePower
content_summary: This model calculates internal energy using a power function of temperature.
content:
Internal energy using a power function of temperature.

$$e_T(T) = C_v(T)T \quad (5.9)$$

$$C_v(T) = C_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)^{n0} \quad (5.10)$$

table.Description: Reference heat capacity at constant volume [J/kg/K]

title: eIcoTabulated
content_summary: The incompressible tabulated thermodynamic model calculates temperature by looking up internal energy from a one-dimensional table based on temperature. Specific heat is calculated using finite difference derivatives of the table data. A heat of formation must also be provided.
content: The incompressible tabulated thermodynamic model can be used to calculate the temperature using temperature to lookup the internal energy. The data is read from a one dimensional table. The specific heat are calculated using finite difference derivatives to the tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x” and ”f” names are ”T” and ”e” respectively. A heat of formation, ”Hf”, must also be provided.

title: eTabulated
content_summary: The tabulated thermodynamic model calculates temperature by looking up internal energy from a two-dimensional table based on density and temperature. Specific heat is calculated using finite difference derivatives of the table data. A heat of formation must be provided, and the equation of state contribution to internal energy must be included in the tables.
content:
The tabulated thermodynamic model can be used to calculate the temperature using density and temperature to find internal energy. The data is read from a two dimensional table. The specific heat are calculated using finite difference derivatives to the tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x”, ”y”, and ”f” names are ”rho”, ”T”, and ”e” respectively. A heat of formation, ”Hf”, must also be provided.

NOTE: The equation of state contribution of internal energy is not included so this must be included in the tables provided.

title: hConst
content_summary: This model calculates enthalpy using a constant value for the specific heat at constant pressure.
content:
Enthalpy is calculated using a constant value of specific heat at constant pressure.

$$h_T(T) = C_p(T - T_{ref}) + H_{s,ref} \quad (5.11)$$

title: hPolynomial
content_summary: This model calculates enthalpy using a polynomial with powers of temperature.
content:
Enthalpy is calculated using polynomial with powers of T.

$$h_T(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} C_{p,i}T^i \quad (5.12)$$

table.Description: Specific heat at constant pressure polynomial coefficients

title: hPower
content_summary: This model calculates internal energy using a power function of temperature.
content:
Internal energy using a power function of temperature.

$$h_T(T) = C_p(T)T \quad (5.13)$$

$$C_v(T) = C_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)^{n0} \quad (5.14)$$

table.Description: Reference heat capacity at constant volume [J/kg/K]

title: hIcoTabulated
content_summary: The incompressible tabulated thermodynamic model calculates temperature by looking up enthalpy from a one-dimensional table based on temperature. Specific heat is calculated using finite difference derivatives of the table data. A heat of formation must also be provided.
content: The incompressible tabulated thermodynamic model can be used to calculate the temperature using temperature to lookup the enthalpy. The data is read from a one dimensional table. The specific heat are calculated using finite difference derivatives to the tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x” and ”f” names are ”T” and ”h” respectively. A heat of formation, ”Hf”, must also be provided.

title: hTabulated
content_summary: The tabulated thermodynamic model calculates temperature by looking up enthalpy from a two-dimensional table based on density and temperature. Specific heat is calculated using finite difference derivatives of the table data. A heat of formation must be provided, and the equation of state contribution to internal energy must be included in the tables.
content:
The tabulated thermodynamic model can be used to calculate the temperature using density and temperature to find enthalpy. The data is read from a two dimensional table. The specific heat are calculated using finite difference derivatives to the tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x”, ”y”, and ”f” names are ”rho”, ”T”, and ”h” respectively. A heat of formation, ”Hf”, must also be provided.

NOTE: The equation of state contribution of internal energy is not included so this must be included in the tables provided.

title: JANAF
content_summary: The JANAF thermodynamic model represents enthalpy using a temperature-based polynomial (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b). It requires seven coefficients for both low and high temperature states, as well as high, low, and switching temperatures. Currently, it is only available for ideal gases.
content: The JANAF thermodynamic model can be used in which the enthalpy is represented by a temperature based polynomial (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b). Seven coefficients must be specified for both the low temperature and high temperature states, as well as the high, low, and switching temperatures. Currently, this model is only available for ideal gases.

title: Equation of states
content_summary: This section explains that to handle a variety of equations of state, both temperature-based and internal energy-based (e.g., Mie-Grüneisen form) equations of state can be used.
content: In order to more easily handle a variety of equations of state, both temperature-based equations of state and internal energy based equations of state (e.g. in Mie-Grüneisen form) can be used.

title: rhoConst
content_summary: For a solid phase, this model assumes a constant density and maintains a reference pressure. Since a solid thermodynamic model is never used to transport mass, the reference pressure is arbitrary and set to 0 Pa by default.
content: For a solid phase, it is assumed to have a constant density, ρ0, and maintains a reference pressure pref. Because a solid thermodynamic model is never used to transport mass, the reference pressure is arbitrary and therefore set to 0 [Pa] by default.

title: Internal energy based (Mie-Grüneisen form)
content_summary: This section explains that the Mie-Grüneisen form calculates pressure using two contributions: an ideal gas term and a deviation term. The Grüneisen coefficient and a pressure deviation term, both specific to the equation of state, are used to define the pressure and the speed of sound.
content:
The Mie-Grüneisen form uses two contributions to calculate the pressure, and ideal gas term contribution, and a deviation term.

$$p = (\Gamma_i - 1)\rho_i e - \Pi_i \quad (5.15)$$

Γi, also called the Grüneisen coefficient, and Πi is the pressure deviation from an ideal gas and is specific to the equation of state. Using these quantities, the speed of sound is written as

$$c_i^2 = \frac{\Gamma_i p + \Pi_i}{\rho_i} - \delta_i(\Gamma_i - 1) \quad (5.16)$$

where δi is defined

$$\delta_i = -\frac{[p + \Pi_i]\Gamma_i'}{[\Gamma_i - 1]^2} + \frac{\Pi_i'}{\Gamma_i - 1} \quad (5.17)$$

and where Γ'i and Π'i are the derivatives with respect to the phase density.

title: idealGas
content_summary: For an ideal gas, this model defines the pressure and represents it in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
For an ideal gas, the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = (\gamma_i - 1)\rho_i e_i \quad (5.18)$$

The functions as defined to represent an ideal gas in Mie-Grüneisen form are:

$$\Gamma_i = \gamma_i \quad (5.19)$$

$$\Pi_i = 0 \quad (5.20)$$

$$\delta_i = 0 \quad (5.21)$$

title: stiffenedGas
content_summary: For a material obeying the stiffened EOS, this model defines the pressure based on material properties (Zheng et al., 2011) and represents it in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
For a material obeying the stiffened EOS, the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = (\gamma_i - 1)\rho_i e_i - \gamma_i a_i \quad (5.22)$$

where ai and γi are material properties (Zheng et al., 2011). In the Mie-Grüneisen form

$$\Gamma_i = \gamma_i \quad (5.23)$$

$$\Pi_i = \gamma_i a_i \quad (5.24)$$

and

$$\delta_i = 0 \quad (5.25)$$

title: (linearTillotson
content_summary: The linear Tillotson equation of state (Wardlaw et al., 1998) defines pressure based on material coefficients and reference conditions. A cavitation pressure is used as a minimum pressure when the density is significantly below the reference density. The model is also represented in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
The linear Tillotson equation of state was presented by (Wardlaw et al., 1998), and the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = p_{0,i} + \omega_i\rho_i(e_T - e_{0,i}) + A_i\mu + B_i\mu^2 + C_i\mu^3 \quad (5.26)$$

where Ai, Bi, Ci, and ωi are material coefficients, and p0,i and e0,i are the reference pressure and internal energy. µ is defined as ρi/ρ0,i − 1. The cavitation pressure, pcav is used as the minimum pressure which occurs when the density is sufficiently below the reference density.

In the Mie-Grüneisen form

$$\Gamma_i = \omega_i + 1 \quad (5.27)$$

and

$$\Pi_i = \omega_i\rho_i e_{0,i} - p_{0,i} - A_i\mu - B_i\mu^2 - C_i\mu^3 \quad (5.28)$$

and

$$\delta_i = e_{0,i} - \frac{A + 2B\mu + 3.0C\mu^2}{\rho_{0,i}\omega_i} \quad (5.29)$$

title: Tillotson
content_summary: The Tillotson equation of state, originally developed for metals (Tillotson, 1962), defines pressure based on material coefficients and reference conditions. A cavitation pressure is used as a minimum pressure for low densities. The model is also represented in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
The Tillotson equation of state was originally developed for metals by Tillotson (1962), and the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = \max\left(\rho_i e \left(a + \frac{b}{\frac{e}{e_{0,i}\eta^2} + 1}\right) + A_i\mu + B_i\mu^2, p_{cav}\right) \quad (5.30)$$

where Ai, Bi, Ci, and ωi are material coefficients, and p0,i and e0,i are the reference pressure and internal energy (Wardlaw et al., 1998). µ is defined as ρi/ρ0,i − 1. The cavitation pressure, pcav is used as the minimum pressure which occurs when the density is sufficiently below the reference density. In the Mie-Grüneisen form

$$\Gamma_i = \frac{a_i x + b_i e_{0,i} \rho_i^2}{x} - \frac{b e e_{0,i}(\rho_i\rho_{0,i})^2}{x^2} + 1 \quad (5.31)$$

where

$$x_i = e\rho_{0,i}^2 + e_{0,i}\rho_i^2 \quad (5.32)$$

and

$$\Pi_i = -A_i\mu - B_i\mu^2 - b_i \frac{(e\rho_i\rho_{0,i})^2 e_{0,i}\rho_i}{(e\rho_{0,i}^2 + e_{0,i}\rho_i^2)^2} \quad (5.33)$$

and δi is calculated using (5.17)

title: Tait
content_summary: For a material obeying the stiffened EOS, this model defines the pressure based on material properties (Zheng et al., 2011) and represents it in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
For a material obeying the stiffened EOS, the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = (\gamma_i - 1)\rho_i e_i - \gamma_i(b_i - a_i) \quad (5.34)$$

where ai, bi, and γi are material properties (Zheng et al., 2011). In the Mie-Grüneisen form

$$\Gamma_i = \gamma_i \quad (5.35)$$

$$\Pi_i = \gamma_i(b_i - a_i) \quad (5.36)$$

and

$$\delta_i = 0 \quad (5.37)$$

title: vanderWaals
content_summary: For a gas described by the generalized van der Waals equation of state (Zheng et al., 2011), this model defines the pressure and represents it in Mie-Grüneisen form.
content:
For a gas described by the generalized van der Waals equation of state (Zheng et al., 2011), the pressure is defined as

$$p_i = \frac{\gamma_i - 1}{1 - b_i\rho_i}(\rho_i e_i + a_i\rho_i^2) - (a_i\rho_i^2 + c_i) \quad (5.38)$$

Putting the pressure in the Mie-Grüneisen form, we obtain

$$\Gamma_i = \frac{\gamma_i - 1}{1 - b_i\rho_i} + 1 \quad (5.39)$$

$$\Pi_i = \left(1 - \frac{\gamma_i - 1}{1 - b_i\rho_i}\right)a_i\rho_i^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_i - 1}{1 - b_i\rho_i} + 1\right)c_i \quad (5.40)$$

and

$$\delta_i = -b_i\frac{p_i + a_i\rho_i^2}{\gamma_i - 1} + \left(\frac{1 - b_i\rho_i}{\gamma - 1} - 1\right)2a_i\rho_i \quad (5.41)$$

table.Description: Specific volume excluded due to particle volume [kg−1 m3]

title: LSZK
content_summary: The Landau, Stanyukovich, Zeldovich, and Kompaneets equation of state is a simple model for describing an ideal explosive (Lutzky, 1964; Needham, 2018), with a form similar to a stiffened gas or Tait EOS.
content:
The Landau, Stanyukovich, Zeldovich, and Kompaneets equation of state is a simple model for describing an ideal explosive (Lutzky, 1964; Needham, 2018). It takes a form similar to a stiffened gas or Tait EOS, with

$$\Gamma_i = \gamma_i \quad (5.42)$$

$$\Pi_i = a_i\rho_i^{b_i} \quad (5.43)$$

and

$$\delta_i = 0 \quad (5.44)$$

title: JWL
content_summary: The Jones-Wilkins-Lee (JWL) EOS is often used for energetic materials (Zheng et al., 2011) and has a defined pressure deviation. It can also be used for the unreacted state with a detonating model of type solidJWL, where the initial energy is calculated for zero pressure at the reference density, and thermal energy is not considered.
content:
The more complicated Jones Wilkins Lee EOS is often used to define energetic materials (Zheng et al., 2011), and has a pressure deviation given by

$$\Pi_i = A_i e^{-R_{1,i}V}\left(\frac{\omega_i}{R_{1,i}V} - 1\right) + B_i e^{-R_{2,i}V}\left(\frac{\omega_i}{R_{2,i}V} - 1\right) - \rho_i\omega_i e_{0,i} \quad (5.45)$$

where V = ρ0,i/ρi.

$$\Gamma_{r,i} = \omega_i + 1 \quad (5.46)$$

and

$$\delta_{r,i} = A_i e^{-\frac{R_{1,i}\rho_{0,i}}{\rho_i}} \left[ \omega_i\left(\frac{1}{R_{1,i}\rho_{0,i}} + \frac{1}{\rho_i}\right) - \frac{R_{1,i}\rho_{0,i}}{\rho_i^2} \right] \frac{1}{\omega_i} + B_i e^{-\frac{R_{2,i}\rho_{0,i}}{\rho_i}} \left[ \omega_i\left(\frac{1}{R_{2,i}\rho_{0,i}} + \frac{1}{\rho_i}\right) - \frac{R_{2,i}\rho_{0,i}}{\rho_i^2} \right] \frac{1}{\omega_i} - e_{0,i} \quad (5.47)$$

NOTE: The JWL model can also be used to describe the unreacted state if a detonating model is used with the solidJWL type. For the solid case, e0,i is calculated so that there is zero pressure at the reference density, and uses an offset pressure, ”pRef”. Thermal energy is also not considered for the solid case

table.Description: Offset energy (0 by default for gas) [J/Kg]
Offset pressure (only for solid, 0 by default) [Pa]

title: CochranChan
content_summary: The Cochran-Chan EOS can be used to describe a solid material (Zheng et al., 2011), with a given reference pressure and functions defined for the Mie-Grüneisen equations.
content:
The Cochran Chan EOS can be used to describe a solid material (Zheng et al., 2011), and has a reference pressure given by

$$p_{ref,i} = A_i V_i^{1-E_{1,i}} - B_i V_i^{1-E_{2,i}} \quad (5.48)$$

The functions used to defined the Mie-Grüneisen equations are

$$\Gamma_i = \Gamma_{0,i} + 1, \quad (5.49)$$

$$\Pi_i = \Gamma_{0,i}\rho_i\left(-\frac{A_i}{(E_{1,i} - 1)\rho_{0,i}}V_i^{1-E_{1,i}} + \frac{B_i}{(E_{2,i} - 1)\rho_{0,i}}V_i^{1-E_{2,i}} - e_{0,i}\right) - p_{ref,i} \quad (5.50)$$

and

$$\delta_i = \frac{A_i}{E_{1,i}}\left[\frac{E_{1,i}V_i^{-E_{1,i}}(E_{1,i} - \Gamma_{0,i} - 1)}{\rho_i} + \frac{\Gamma_{0,i}}{\rho_{0,i}}\right]\frac{1}{\Gamma_{0,i}} + \frac{B_i}{E_{2,i}}\left[\frac{E_{2,i}V_i^{-E_{2,i}}(E_{2,i} - \Gamma_{0,i} - 1)}{\rho_i} + \frac{\Gamma_{0,i}}{\rho_{0,i}}\right]\frac{1}{\Gamma_{0,i}} - e_{0,i} \quad (5.51)$$

title: DoanNickel
content_summary: The Doan-Nickel EOS accounts for changes in air composition and specific heat ratio at high temperatures due to molecular dissociation (Doan and Nickel, 1963). It can currently only be used with inviscid fluids and a constant specific heat at constant volume (eConst). The specific heat is a function of density and internal energy, and all coefficients are hard-coded for standard air.
content:
The Doan-Nickel EOS includes changes in the composition of air at high temperatures and the changing specific heat ratio due to dissociation of molecules Doan and Nickel (1963). Due to the way this model is defined, it can currently only be used with inviscid fluids and a constant specific heat at constant volume (eConst). The specific heat is a function of the density and internal energy and is defined as

$$\Gamma_i = 1 + [0.161 + 0.255e^{\tilde{e}/e_1}f_1 + 0.280e^{\tilde{e}/e_2}(1 - f_1) + 0.137e^{\tilde{e}/e_3}f_2 + 0.050f_3] \left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{\alpha(\rho_i, \tilde{e})} \quad (5.52)$$

where e˜ = e · 10−6. e1 = 4.46, e2 = 6.63, and e3 = 25.5 are the internal energies at which oxygen dissociates, everything is dissociated, and electrons contribute, respectively. ρ0,i is the density of air at sea-level (1.293 [kg/m3], and

$$\alpha(\rho_i, \tilde{e}) = [0.048f_1 + 0.032(1 - f_1)(1 - f_2)] \log_{10}(\tilde{e}) + 0.045f_3 \quad (5.53)$$

with

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{\exp\left(\frac{\tilde{e} - e_{O_2->2O}}{\Delta e_{O_2->2O}}\right) + 1} \quad (5.54)$$

$$e_{O_2->2O} = 8.5 + 0.357\log_{10}\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right) \quad (5.55)$$

$$\Delta e_{O_2->2O} = 0.975\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{0.05} \quad (5.56)$$

$$f_2 = \frac{1}{\exp(\frac{e_{N_2->2N} - \tilde{e}}{\Delta e_{N_2->2N}}) + 1} \quad (5.57)$$

$$e_{N_2->2N} = 45.0 \left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{0.0157} \quad (5.58)$$

$$\Delta e_{N_2->2N} = 4.0 \left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{0.085} \quad (5.59)$$

$$f_3 = \frac{1}{\exp(\frac{e_e - \tilde{e}}{\Delta e_e}) + 1} \quad (5.60)$$

$$e_e = 160.0 \quad (5.61)$$

and

$$\Delta e_e = 6.0 \quad (5.62)$$

Due to the complexity of the model, and the fact that it was specifically developed as a model for air, all coefficients are hard-coded and cannot be changed. This equation of state should only be used to represent standard air.

title: Temperature-based
content_summary: This section explains that for some equations of state that cannot be converted to an internal energy-based form, a temperature-based approach is used. In this case, the Grüneisen coefficient is defined to calculate the shared pressure, and the speed of sound is defined accordingly.
content:
While it is preferable to use an internal energy based equation of state for compressible flows, some equations of state cannot be converted, and therefore use the local temperature to define the thermodynamic pressure. Generally the Grüneisen coefficient used to calculate the shared pressure is defined as

$$\Gamma_i = \frac{v_i}{C_v}\frac{\partial p}{\partial T} \quad (5.63)$$

where Cv is the specific heat at constant volume, and the speed of sound is defined

$$c_i^2 = v_i^2\left[\left(\frac{\partial p_i}{\partial T}\right)^2 \frac{T}{C_v} - \frac{\partial p}{\partial v_i}\right] \quad (5.64)$$

Unless otherwise specified, these relations are used to calculate the relevant quantities.

title: perfectGas
content_summary: The perfect gas equation of state uses the standard ideal gas pressure relation but allows for non-constant heat capacity models by not holding the specific heat ratio constant.
content:
The perfect gas equation of state uses the standard ideal gas pressure relation, however the specific heat ratio is not held constant, allowing for the use of non-constant heat capacity models.

$$p_i = RT\rho_i \quad (5.65)$$

title: JWLC
content_summary: The C-form of the Jones-Wilkins-Lee equation of state (Souers et al., 2000) does not depend on temperature or internal energy. It defines pressure and the speed of sound with specific formulas, and uses the same Grüneisen coefficient as the standard JWL equation of state.
content:
The C-form of the Jones Wilkins Lee equation of state (Souers et al., 2000) does not have a dependence on the temperature or internal energy and has a pressure defined as

$$p_i = A_i e^{-R_{1,i}V} + B_i e^{-R_{2,i}V} + \frac{C_i}{V^{1+\omega_i}} \quad (5.66)$$

the speed of sound if defined

$$c_i^2 = \frac{V^2}{\rho_{0,i}}\left(A_i e^{-R_{1,i}V} + B_i e^{-R_{2,i}V} + \frac{C_i(1 + \omega_i)}{V^{2+\omega_i}}\right) \quad (5.67)$$

The Grüneisen coefficient is the same as the standard JWL equation of state.

title: AbelNobel
content_summary: The Abel-Nobel equation of state is commonly used for modeling explosive materials, particularly propellants (Johnston, 2005). Unlike the JWL and BKW models, it uses temperature to define pressure.
content:
The equation of state of Abel-Nobel is commonly used for modeling simulate explosive materials, specifically for propellants (Johnston, 2005). Unlike the JWL equation of state, the BKW models uses the temperature, rather than the internal energy, to define the pressure.

$$p_i = \frac{RT\rho_i}{1 - b_i\rho_i} \quad (5.68)$$

where b is the co-volume of the fluid.

title: BKW
content_summary: The Becker-Kistiakowsky-Wilson (BKW) equation of state is commonly used to simulate explosive materials (Mader, 1963). Like the Abel-Nobel model, it uses temperature to define pressure.
content:
The Becker-Kistiakowsky-Wilson equation of state is another equation of state that is commonly used to simulate explosive materials (Mader, 1963). Unlike the JWL equation of state, the BKW models uses the temperature, rather than the internal energy, to define the pressure.

$$p_i = RT\rho_i(1 + xe^{\beta x}) \quad (5.69)$$

where

$$x = \frac{\kappa k \rho_i}{W_i(T + \theta)^a} \quad (5.70)$$

and Wi is the molecular weight in [kg/kmol].

title: BWR
content_summary: The Benedict-Webb-Rubin (BWR) equation of state (Benedict et al., 1942) describes real gases using a set of eight experimentally determined coefficients to define the pressure.
content:
The Benedict-Webb-Rubin equation of state was developed by (Benedict et al., 1942) to describe real gases using a set of eight coefficients that must be experimentally determined. The pressure is defined as

$$p_i = RT\rho_i + \left(B_0RT - A_0 - \frac{C_0}{T^2}\right)\rho_i^2 + (bRT - a)\rho_i^3 + a\alpha\rho_i^6 + \frac{c\rho_i^3}{T^2}(1 + \gamma\rho_i^2)e^{-\gamma\rho_i^2} \quad (5.71)$$

title: Murnaghan
content_summary: The Murnaghan equation of state is used for solid material and has no temperature dependence (Souers et al., 2000). The pressure is a function of density only.
content:
The Murnaghan (or Munahan) equation of state is used for solid material and has no temperature dependence (Souers et al., 2000). The pressure is a function of only the density and is defined as

$$p_i = p_{ref} + \frac{1}{\kappa n}\left(\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^n - 1\right) \quad (5.72)$$

table.Description: Bulk modulus (can be used in place of κ) [Pa]

title: tabulatedEOS
content_summary: The tabulated equation of state allows for pressure to be calculated from two-dimensional tables of density and temperature, with various options for modifiers and interpolation schemes.
content: The tabulated equation of state can be used to calculate the pressure using the density and temperature. The data is read from a two dimensional tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x”, ”y”, and ”f” names are ”rho”, ”T”, and ”p” respectively.

title: BirchMurnaghan2
content_summary: The second-order Birch-Murnaghan equation of state is the second-order expansion of the Birch-Murnaghan model, and defines the pressure accordingly (Zheng and Zhao, 2016).
content:
The second order Birch-Murnaghan equation of state is the second order expansion of the Birch-Murnaghan model. The pressure is defined as

$$p_i = p_{ref} + \frac{3K_0}{2K_0'}\left[\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{7/3} - \left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{5/3}\right] \quad (5.73)$$

Many coefficients can be found for this in Zheng and Zhao (2016).

title: BirchMurnaghan3
content_summary: The third-order Birch-Murnaghan equation of state is the third-order expansion of the Birch-Murnaghan model, and defines the pressure accordingly (Zheng and Zhao, 2016).
content:
The Third order Birch-Murnaghan equation of state is the third order expansion of the Birch-Murnaghan (see Zheng and Zhao (2016)). The pressure is defined as

$$p_i = p_{ref} + \frac{3K_0}{2K_0'}\left[\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{7/3} - \left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{5/3}\right] \times \left\{1 + 0.75(K_0' - 4)\left[\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\right)^{2/3} - 1\right]\right\} \quad (5.74)$$

table.Description: Change in bulk modulus w.r.t. pressure [ ]
