title: eTabulated/tabulatedMG
content_summary: In addition to the previously mentioned models, tabulated models for equations of state and thermodynamics can be used. This is useful for simulating real gases at high temperatures and pressures where standard models are insufficient. Any combination of pressure and temperature tables can be used, as long as they are a function of density and internal energy. The internal energy is initialized using a reverse lookup from a given pressure and density. This option is currently only available with const, icoTabulated, and tabulated transport models.
content:
In addition to the previously mentioned equations of state and thermodynamic models, tabulated models may also be used. This is useful when simulating real gasses, and high temperatures and pressures when standard models are no longer sufficient. Any combination of pressure and temperature tables can be used, as long as they are a function of density and internal energy (x and y, respectively). The internal energy is initialized using a reverse look up given a pressure and a density. See 12.1 for more information on usage.

NOTE: Because of the way the selection of models is handled, this option will not show up as an available option if other models are used. Currently only const, icoTabulated, and tabulated transport models are available. An example is shown below:

title: Fluid Transport
content_summary: This section explains that transport models are used to define quantities like viscosity and thermal diffusion. Currently, only Newtonian viscosity models are available. All transport models, except for the tabulated methods, are ported from OpenCFD Ltd. (2018b).
content: The transport models are used to define quantities such as viscosity and thermal diffusion. Only Newtonian viscosity models are currently available. All transport models, with the exception of the tabulated methods ported from OpenCFD Ltd. (2018b).

title: const
content_summary: This model uses constant values for viscosity and Prandtl number.
content:
Constant values for viscosity (µ) and Prandtl number are used.

$$\kappa = \mu C_p / Pr \quad (5.75)$$

title: sutherland
content_summary: This model calculates viscosity using Sutherland's formula.
content:
Viscosity is calculated using Sutherland’s formula for viscosity

$$\mu(T) = A_s \frac{\sqrt{T}}{1 + T_s/T} \quad (5.76)$$

$$\kappa = \mu C_v\left(1.32 + \frac{1.77R}{C_v}\right) \quad (5.77)$$

title: logPolynomial
content_summary: This transport package uses polynomial functions of the natural logarithm of temperature for the natural logarithm of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity.
content:
Transport package using polynomial functions of the natural logarithm of temperature for the natural logarithm of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity:

$$\log(\mu(T)) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \mu_i(\log(T))^i \quad (5.78)$$

$$\log(\kappa(T)) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \kappa_i(\log(T))^i \quad (5.79)$$

title: polynomial
content_summary: This transport package uses polynomial functions of temperature for dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity.
content:
Transport package using polynomial functions of temperature for the dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity:

$$\mu(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \mu_i T^i \quad (5.80)$$

$$\kappa(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \kappa_i T^i \quad (5.81)$$

title: WLF
content_summary: This transport package uses the Williams-Landel-Ferry model for the viscosity of polymer melts (Williams et al., 1955).
content:
Transport package using the Williams-Landel-Ferry model for viscosity of polymer melts (Williams et al., 1955):

$$\mu(T) = \mu_0 \exp\left(-\frac{C_1(T - T_r)}{C_2 + T - T_r}\right) \quad (5.82)$$

$$\kappa(T) = C_p(T)\frac{\mu(T)}{Pr} \quad (5.83)$$

title: icoTabulated
content_summary: The tabulated, incompressible transport model calculates viscosity and conductivity using only temperature, with data read from two one-dimensional tables.
content: The tabulated, incompressible transport model can be used to calculate the viscosity and conductivity using only the temperature. The data is read from two one dimensional tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x” and ”f” names are ”T” and ”mu”/”kappa” respectively.

title: tabulated
content_summary: The tabulated transport model calculates viscosity and conductivity using density and temperature, with data read from two two-dimensional tables.
content: The tabulated transport model can be used to calculate the viscosity and conductivity using the density and temperature. The data is read from two, two dimensional tables. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x”, ”y”, and ”f” names are ”rho”, ”T”, and ”mu”/”kappa” respectively.

title: Solid Transport
content_summary: This section explains that transport models are used to define the thermal conductivity of a material. All transport models, except for the tabulated methods, are ported from OpenCFD Ltd. (2018b).
content: The transport models are used to define the thermal conductivity of the material. All transport models, with the exception of the tabulated methods are ported from OpenCFD Ltd. (2018b).

title: constIso
content_summary: This model uses a constant, scalar (isotropic) value for thermal conductivity.
content: A constant, scalar value (isotropic), is used for for thermal conductivity.

title: constAnIso
content_summary: This model uses a constant, vector (anisotropic) value for thermal conductivity.
content: A constant, vector value (anistropic), is used for thermal conductivity.

title: exponential
content_summary: This model calculates thermal conductivity using an exponential function.
content:
Thermal conductivity is calculated using an exponential function

$$\kappa(T) = \kappa_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)^{n0} \quad (5.84)$$

title: polynomial
content_summary: This model calculates thermal conductivity using polynomial functions of temperature.
content:
Thermal conductivity is calculated using polynomial functions of temperature.

$$\kappa(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \kappa_i T^i \quad (5.85)$$

title: tabulatedIso
content_summary: The tabulated, isotropic solid transport model calculates conductivity using temperature, with data read from a one-dimensional table.
content: The tabulated, isotroptic solid transport model can be used to calculate the conductivity using the temperature. The data is read from a one dimensional table. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x” and ”f” names are ”T” and ”kappa” respectively.

title: tabulatedAnIso
content_summary: The tabulated, anisotropic solid transport model calculates conductivity using temperature, with data read from a one-dimensional table.
content: The tabulated, anisotroptic solid transport model can be used to calculate the conductivity using the temperature. The data is read from a one dimensional table. See Sec. 12.1 for more information on the available options for modifiers and interpolation schemes. The ”x” and ”f” names are ”T” and ”kappa” respectively.

title: Basic thermodynamic model
content_summary: This section explains that the basicFluid and basicSolid models ('type' basic) use a transport model, a thermodynamic model, and an equation of state to describe a non-reacting phase.
content: The basicFluid and basicSolid models (”type” basic) uses a transport model, thermodynamic model, and equation of state to describe a phase. The basic model should be used for non-reacting phases.

title: Detonating thermodynamic model
content_summary: This section describes the 'detonating' thermodynamic model, which transitions a material from an unreacted to a reacted state using an activation model. Each state is described by its own basic thermodynamic model, allowing for different coefficients for properties like viscosity or specific heats. Afterburn models can also be used to add energy after the initial detonation. This methodology is also applied to solid phases where energy is released locally to increase thermal energy. An ideal gas can be used as a simple option if the solid properties of the unreacted material are unknown.
content:
In order to better describe a detonating material, a ’detonating’ thermodynamic model can be used. This model transitions between an unreacted state and a reacted state using an activation model. Each state (unreacted and reacted) is described by its own basic thermodynamic model, allowing for different models and coefficients to be used for values such as viscosity or specific heats. In addition to the activation model, afterburn models can also be used to add additional energy into the system after the initial detonation reaction has taken place. This same methodology is applied to solid phases where energy is released locally to increase the thermal energy (internal energy). Only activation models are currenltly available. Some standard combinations of unreacted and reacted equations of states include the JWL++ (Murnaghn and JWLC), and the blendedJWL (solidJWL and JWL).

NOTE: Whilst it will not give the correct temperature, using an ideal gas is a simple option if the solid properties of the unreacted material are not known.

title: Activation Models
content_summary: This section describes different models available to approximate the speed at which solid reactants become product gases, including linear, pressure-based, and Arrhenius rate reaction models. These models use a reaction progress variable to describe the state of cell activation. To ensure numerical stability, the initial energy is defined by the unreacted equation of state, and reaction energy is added based on the rate of change of the reaction progress variable. Phase properties are calculated by blending the unreacted and reacted states. Detonation points can be set for any activation model to specify when and where cells are fully activated. The transport of the reaction progress variable is also described.
content:
In order to accurately model the release of energy, different models can be used to approximate the speed at which the solid reactants become the product gases. The currently available models include the linear model, a pressure based model, and finally an Arrhenius rate reaction. Theses models all use a reaction progress variable, λi, to describe the state of individual cell activation. Additionally, in order to add numerical stability, the initial energy is defined using the unreacted equation of state, and the reaction energy, E0, is added based on the time rate of change of the reaction progress variable, i.e.

$$\dot{E} = \frac{E_0\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\frac{d\lambda}{dt} \quad (5.86)$$

The phase properties are calculated using

$$x_i = \lambda_i^l x_{r,i} + (1 - \lambda_i^l)x_{u,i}) \quad (5.87)$$

where xr,i is the reacted phase quantity, xu,i is the unreacted phase quantity, and l is the exponent used to blend the unreacted and reacted states (lambdaPow). By default l is one. The advection scheme and limiters for λi need to be specified in the fvSchemes in the same manner as the other transported quantities. The setting of detonation points can be done for any activation model. By setting detonation points, the cells within the charge with the radii specified are set to full activated when run time reaches the given delays. If no radii are provided, only the cell containing the point will be activated. Additionally, delays can be used to specify the time at which the given point and cells within the radius are activated. For a single detonation point, the ”useCOM” option can be used to calculate the center of mass of an individual charge. Transport of the reaction progress variable is described by the transport equation

$$\frac{\partial \alpha_i\rho_i\lambda_i}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u} \lambda_i) = \alpha_i\rho_i \dot{R}_i \quad (5.88)$$

where Ri is the reaction rate determined by the model of choice.

table.Description: Initiation radii around detonation points (default is 0) [m]
Delay time of detonation points (default is 0) [s]
Is the center of mass used as the detation point (default is no)

title: none
content_summary: The 'none' activation model assumes all cells are activated at the start of the simulation, which is equivalent to an infinitely fast detonation velocity. Delays and detonation points are not applicable, and there is no transport of the reaction progress variable.
content: The none activation model assumes that all cells are activated when the simulation begins. This is the equivalent to an infinitely fast detonation velocity. The use of delays and detonation points are not valid here because the detonation is assumed to be instantaneous. There is no transport of the reaction progress variable.

title: linear
content_summary: The simplest activation model uses a list of detonation points and a constant detonation velocity to determine which cells have been activated. For multi-point detonations, the actual reaction progress is the maximum value from all points.
content:
The simplest activation model uses a list of detonation points and a constant detonation velocity to determine which cells have been activated. This gives

$$\lambda_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } |\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{det,i,j}| < v_{det,i}t \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (5.89)$$

where x is the cell center, xdet,i,j is the jth detonation point, vdet,i is the detonation velocity, and t is the time. In order to account for multi-point detonation, the actual λ is defined as

$$\lambda_i = \max(\lambda_{i,j}) \quad (5.90)$$

table.Description: Speed of propagation within the material [m/s]

title: programmedIgnition
content_summary: The programmedIgnition activation model is more complex than the linear model and includes an option for activation due to material compression, based on the work of Giroux (1973). The reaction progress variable is defined by a combination of a compression model and a modified linear activation model.
content:
The programmedIgnition activation model is slightly more complex than the linear activation model and includes the option to use activation due to compression of material, and is based on the work of Giroux (1973). The reaction progress variable is defined using a combination of two models, the compression

$$\lambda_{\beta,i} = \frac{1 - \frac{\rho_{0,i}}{\rho_i}}{1 - V_{CJ,i}} \quad (5.91)$$

where VCJ,i is the Chapman-Jougnuet specific volume which is computed using the Chapman-Jouguet pressure. The reference density, ρ0,i is read from the product equation of state. The programmed definition of the reaction progress variable is a modified version of the linear activation, where a cell reacts at a time-rate determined by the detonation velocity and the local grid size

$$\lambda_{programmed,i} = \max(t - t_{ign,j}, 0) \frac{v_{det}}{1.5dx} \quad (5.92)$$

where tign is the time of ignition determined by the physical location and the detonation velocity, and dx is the local grid size. If the beta burn option is used, λi = λβ,i; if the programmed burn option is used, λi = λprogrammed,i; and if the programmedBeta burn option is used λi = max(λi,β, λprogrammed,i).

table.Description: Speed of propagation within the material [m/s]
Burn model used (beta, programmed, or programmedBeta)

title: pressureBased
content_summary: A more advanced activation model that uses the local cell pressure to follow an evolution equation. The first term in the equation handles the initial detonation based on material compression, while the following two terms account for subsequent reactions based on local pressure. These terms can be activated or deactivated by specifying the minimum and maximum lambda values for each stage.
content:
A more advanced activation model uses the local cell pressure, determined by the equation of state, and follows the evolution equation

$$\dot{R}_i = I\left(\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}} - 1 - a\right)^x(1 - \lambda)^b + G_1(1 - \lambda)^c\lambda^d p^y + G_2(1 - \lambda)^e\lambda^f p^z \quad (5.93)$$

The first term is responsible for the initial detonation based on the compression of the material. The following two terms account for the subsequent reaction based on the local pressure. All terms can be turned on or off by specifying the minimum and maximum values of lambda at which the stages occur.

table.Description: Maximum value of λ that the ignition term is used [ ]
Minimum value of λ that the first step is used [ ] (default 0)
Maximum value of λ that the first step is used [ ] (default 1)
Minimum value of λ that the second step is used [ ] (default 0)
Maximum value of λ that the second step is used [ ] (default 1)
Coefficient used to scale pressure [ ] (default is 1)

title: ArrheniusRate
content_summary: A more physical representation of the activation rate that uses the local temperature, which is defined by the internal energy and specific heat of the mixture. The reaction progress variable's evolution equation is given, with the particle diameter determined by a chosen diameter model.
content:
A more physical representation of the activation rate uses the local temperature, defined by the internal energy and the specific heat of the mixture. The evolution equation for the reaction progress variable is

$$\dot{R}_i = (1 - \lambda) \begin{cases} 0 & T < T_{ign} \\ d_p^2 A_{low} \exp(\frac{E_{a,low}}{RT}) & T_{ign} \le T < T_s \\ A_{high} \exp(\frac{E_{a,high}}{RT}) & T \ge T_s \end{cases} \quad (5.94)$$

Where the particle diameter is determined by the chosen diameter model (see Sec. 9), and R is the ideal gas constant. This model is still in development and has not been validated

table.Description: Diameter model used to determine the particle diameter

title: Afterburn Models
content_summary: This section explains that afterburn models can be used to add energy after the initial charge has been activated, resulting in higher pressure and temperature. This is typical in under-oxygenated or non-ideal explosives that release energy at a later time. The advection scheme and limiters for this process need to be specified in the fvSchemes.
content: When reactions occur over a period of time after the initial charge has been activated, additional energy can be added that results in higher pressure and temperature, and is refered to afterburn. Afterburn is typical in under-oxygenated and/or non-ideal explosives, which may release energy at a later time. The advection scheme and limiters for ci need to be specified in the fvSchemes in the same manner as the other transported quantities.

title: none [default]
content_summary: The default model where no additional energy is added. This model is used if the 'afterburnModel' keyword is not found in the phase dictionary.
content: No additional energy is added. If the keyword ”afterburnModel” is not found in the phase dictionary, this is the model that is used.

title: constant
content_summary: This model adds a constant amount of afterburn energy as specified by the user.
content: A constant amount of afterburn energy is added where Q is specified by the user.

title: linear
content_summary: This model adds afterburn energy linearly between a specified start and end time.
content:
Afterburn energy is added linearly from a start time to an end time where

$$\dot{Q} = \begin{cases} \frac{Q_0}{\Delta t} & \text{if } t_{start} < t < t_{end} \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (5.95)$$

title: Miller
content_summary: The Miller (1995) model uses an evolution equation to determine the fraction of total afterburn energy added due to unburnt material. The reaction progress variable has a value between 0 and 1.
content:
The model of Miller (1995) uses an evolution equation to determine the fraction of the total amount of afterburn energy added to the system due to unburnt material. ci is the reaction progress variable that has a value between 0 and 1, with an evolution equation defined as

$$\frac{\partial \alpha_i\rho_i c_i}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u} c_i) = \alpha_i\rho_i a(1 - c_i)^m(p \times p_{scale})^n \quad (5.96)$$

and the total afterburn energy is found using

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{dc_i}{dt}Q_0 \quad (5.97)$$

title: Multicomponent thermodynamic model
content_summary: The multicomponent fluid thermodynamic model allows for multiple species that share the same equation of state, with coefficients determined by mass averaging. Initial conditions require mass fractions for the species, and a 'defaultSpecie' must be provided in the thermodynamic dictionary to specify a slave species.
content: The multicomponent fluid thermodynamic model allows for the use of multiple species that use the same equation of state by mass averaging equation of state coefficients of the species. The mass fractions of the species are required as initial conditions (Ydefault can be used to give initial conditions for multiple species). Additionally, a ”defaultSpecie” must be provided in the thermodynamic dictionary to specify a slave specie

title: Two/multiphase models
content_summary: To simulate a two-phase or multiphase system, the 'phases' keyword must be defined in the phaseProperties dictionary, listing the phases used. The mixture pressure is defined by a weighted average of the individual phase pressures, which are determined by their respective equations of state. The speed of sound within a given phase is also defined.
content:
To simulate a two-phase or multiphase system, the keyword ”phases” must be defined in the phaseProperties dictionary and defines the list of phases used in a simulation. The selection of a two-phase or multiphase system is determined by the number of defined phases. The mixture pressure is defined by

$$p = \frac{\sum_i \alpha_i \xi_i p_i}{\sum_i \alpha_i \xi_i} \quad (5.98)$$

where

$$\xi_i(\rho_i) = \frac{1}{\Gamma_i - 1} \quad (5.99)$$

and pi is the pressure from a single equation of state (Zheng et al., 2011). The speed of sound within a given phase is given by

$$c_i = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_i y_i \xi_i c_i^2}{\sum_i \xi_i}} \quad (5.100)$$

with

$$y_i = \frac{\alpha_i\rho_i}{\rho} \quad (5.101)$$

Both the initial energy and temperature are calculated using a root finding method (Newton-Raphson by default) that include the contributions of all phases so the calculated values obey the mixture equations. See 12.3 for additional options.

title: Initialization
content_summary: This section describes the initialization of fields, which can be done in several ways, though density, pressure, and temperature must be given as initial conditions. For Mie-Grüneisen equations of state, pressure and density are used by default to initialize internal energy. For temperature-based equations, density and temperature are used. If the 'calculateDensity' keyword is set to 'yes', density is calculated from pressure and temperature before internal energy initialization. If the internal energy field is provided in the initial conditions, pressure and temperature are calculated directly from it and the density.
content: The initialization of fields can be handled in several ways, but ρi, p, and T must be given as initial conditions. Some of these fields may be overwritten, but the boundary conditions are used to construct the conserved quantities. For equations of state in the Mie-Grüneisen form, by default the pressure and density are used to initialize the internal energy. Temperature based equations of state initialize the internal energy using the density and temperature. If the ”calculateDensity” keyword is defined, and is set to ”yes” in the given equation of state dictionary, then the density is calculated from the pressure and temperature prior to the initialization of the internal energy. Each of the initialization methods use a Newton-Raphson root finding method to find the necessary quantities. If the internal energy field is provided in the initial conditions folder, the pressure and temperature fields are calculated directly from the density and internal energy.

title: Example
content_summary: This section provides an example of a single-phase phaseProperties file.
content: An example of a single phase phaseProperties file can be seen below

title: Turbulence
content_summary: This chapter explains that in addition to hyperbolic flux terms, viscous effects can be included in simulations through turbulence models like Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) or Large Eddy simulations (LES). All standard OpenFOAM single-phase turbulence models are available. The chosen model adds transport equations and modifies transport coefficients like viscosity and thermal diffusivity. The available turbulence models are listed and can be found using the '-listMomentumTransportModels' option when running blastFoam. Many of these models require additional fields to be specified in the initial conditions.
content:
In addition to the hyperbolic flux terms, viscous effects can be included in simulations. This includes the use of turbulence models such as Reynolds averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) or Large Eddy simulations (LES) models. All single phase turbulence models standard to OpenFOAM including k-epsilon, k-omega, Smagorinsky, as well as many others are available. The specified model introduces additional transport equations, as well as modifications to transport coefficients like viscosity (ν) and thermal diffusivity (α) which modify the viscous terms in the conservation equations. When the maximum viscosity is zero, the constant/momentumTransport (or constant/turbulence Properties) does not need to be included, however if any phase has viscous terms, this dictionary needs to be included along with the appropriate turbulence model (laminar is the same as no turbulence model). The available turbulence models are listed below, and these can also be found using the ’-listMomentumTransportModels’ option when running blastFoam.

Turbulence models: LES, RAS, laminar (e.g. no model)
laminar models:

• Giesekus
• Stokes (default)
• Maxwell
• generalizedNewtonian

RAS models:

• LRR
• kOmega
• LaunderSharmaKE
• kOmega2006
• RNGkEpsilon
• kOmegaSST
• SSG
• kOmegaSSTLM
• SpalartAllmaras
• kOmegaSSTSAS
• buoyantKEpsilon
• realizableKE
• kEpsilon
• v2f

LES models:

• DeardorffDiffStress
• WALE
• Smagorinsky
• dynamicKEqn
• SpalartAllmarasDDES
• dynamicLagrangian
• SpalartAllmarasDES
• kEqn
• SpalartAllmarasIDDES
• kOmegaSSTDES

NOTE: Many of the turbulence models require additional field (k, epsilon, omega, etc.) to be specified in the initial conditions folder.

title: Burst patches
content_summary: This chapter describes the use of 'bursting' patches, including burst (external), burstCyclic (internal without AMR), and burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields for these patches require an additional 'inactField' entry to specify the boundary condition before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created with any mesh generation technique, but cyclic variations require the createBaffles utility.
content: The ability to have a "bursting" patch can be used with the burst (external patch), burstCyclic (internal with no AMR), or burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields require an additional entry in their dictionaries name ”inactField” which is used to specify the boundary condition of the field before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created using any mesh generation technique, however the cyclic variations require using the createBaffles utility, standard to OpenFOAM. See Sec. 20.5 for an example of how to create and use the patches.

title: Burst models
content_summary: This section explains that the method for calculating when a burst occurs is runtime-selectable, with inputs placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file. The settings can also be added within the patch entry of blockMeshDict or snappyHexMeshDict.
content: The method to calculate where burst occurs is runtime-selectable and the inputs are placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file under the corresponding boundary. This can also be added in either the patch entry within blockMeshDict of snappyHexMeshDict.

table.Description: Can individual face burst (no by default)
Us the difference across coupled patches rather than the maximum value (yes by default)

title: pressure
content_summary: In this model, only pressure is used to determine if a burst occurs.
content: Only pressure is used to determine if burst occurs.

table.Description: Name of the pressure field (p by default)

title: impulse
content_summary: In this model, only impulse is used to determine if a burst occurs. Since an impulse field must be available during the simulation, it is recommended to use the impulse functionObject.
content: Only impulse is used to determine if burst occurs. Because an impulse field must be available while running the simulation, it is recommended to use the impulse functionObject (Sec. 15), otherwise some other method of calculating impulse needs to be used.

table.Description: Name of the impulse field (impulse by default)

title: pressureAndImpulse
content_summary: This model uses a combination of pressure and impulse. A burst occurs if either model's condition is met. Both sets of inputs are required.
content: A combination of the pressure and impulse models are used. If either model results in a burst, then burst occurs. Both sets of inputs are required.

title: field
content_summary: This model uses a list of fields and burst values to determine if a patch has burst. The inputs can be given as a single entry, multiple burst conditions, or as a list of names and values.
content: A list of fields and bust values are used to determine if the patch has burst. The inputs can either be given as a single entry (”burstValue”), multiple burst conditions (”burstValues”), or as a list of names and values (”fields” and ”values”). The latter is used when writing to remove errors in ParaView. See below for an example.

table.Description: Used for single field burst condition [name value]
Used for multiple field burst condition [(name1 value1 name2 value2 ... nameN valueN)]

title: Region models
content_summary: This chapter explains that when multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches to transfer data between fields without requiring special mesh patches. The information is transferred using field-specific boundary conditions, with the interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field specified in the constant/regionProperties file.
content: When multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches which do not require any special mesh patches to transfer data between field. Instead, the information is transferred using field specific boundary conditions. The interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field is specified in the constant/regionProperties file. An example of regionProperties can be seen below

title: Boundary conditions
content_summary: This section describes several boundary conditions that have been implemented to make use of global patches.
content: Several boundary conditions have been implemented that make use of the global patches and are described below.

title: globalMapped
content_summary: The globalMapped boundary condition can be used for both volume and point fields, directly mapping values from one patch to another, with the neighbor patch specified in regionProperties.
content: Both vol and point field can use the globalMapped boundary condition which directly maps values from one patch to another where the neighbour patch is specified in regionProperties.

table.Description: Name of field to map from

title: globalInterpolated
content_summary: The globalInterpolated boundary condition for point fields maps a volume field to a point field, with the neighbor patch specified in regionProperties.
content: Point field can use the globalInterpolated boundary condition which maps a volume field to a point field where the neighbour patch is specified in regionProperties.

table.Description: Name of field to map from

title: globalTemperatureCoupled
content_summary: Using the same methodology as the globalMapped boundary condition, the globalTemperatureCoupled boundary condition computes the temperature of a patch using the neighbor temperature, heat transfer coefficient, and radiation heat flux. An unmapped temperature can be specified for cases where the entire boundary does not overlap the neighbor patch.
content: Using the same methodology as the globalMapped boundary condition, the globalTemperatureCoupled boundary condition computes the temperature of a patch using the neighbour temperature, heat transfer coefficient, and radiation heat flux. Optionally when the entire boundary does not overlap the neighbour patch and unmapped temperature can be specified.

table.Description: Name of neighbour temperature (T by default)
Name of neighbour convective heat transfer coefficient (none by default)
Name of convective heat transfer coefficient (none by default)
Name of neighbour radiation heat flux (none by default)
Name of radiation heat flux (T by default)
Is the temperature gradient limited (no by default)

title: Diameter models
content_summary: This chapter describes the models available for the diameter of a dispersed phase, which is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model and interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver.
content: The diameter of a dispersed phase is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model as well as interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver. Below are is a short description of the models currently available.

title: constant
content_summary: This model uses a constant diameter, and the diameter field is not written.
content:
A constant diameter is used.

NOTE: The diameter field is not written

title: constantMass
content_summary: In this model, the diameter is determined using a reference diameter at a reference density. The mass is defined, and the true diameter is calculated from it.
content:
The diameter is determined using a reference diameter at a reference density. The mass is defined as

$$M = \frac{\pi d_0^3 \rho_0}{6} \quad (9.1)$$

and the true diameter is calculated using

$$d = \left(\frac{6M}{\rho\pi}\right)^{1/3} \quad (9.2)$$

title: reacting
content_summary: The reacting diameter model adds a source term to the diameter transport equation, causing the local diameter to increase or decrease based on a given reaction rate.
content:
The reacting diameter model adds an additional source term in the transport equation for the diameter to increase/decrease the local diameter based on the given reaction rate

$$\frac{\partial d_i}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u}_i \cdot \nabla d_i = k \quad (9.3)$$

where k is the specified reaction rate (see Sec. 9.4)

title: Reaction Rates
content_summary: This section explains that reaction rates are used to give rates based on temperature and/or pressure. There are currently two options: pressure-based and Arrhenius rate.
content: Reaction rates are used to give rates based on temperature and/or pressure currently there are two options, pressure based, and Arrhenius rate

title: pressureBased
content_summary: The pressureBased reaction rate model computes the rate using a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units), and pressure exponent.
content:
The pressureBased reaction rate uses a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units) and pressure exponent to compute the rate

$$k = \beta(\bar{p})^{\alpha} \quad (9.4)$$

and p¯ = p × pscale. For example, if the pressure relation is in MPa, pscale = 1e − 6.

title: Arrhenius
content_summary: This model gives the reaction rate based on the Arrhenius reaction rate formula.
content:
The rate given by the Arrhenius reaction rate is

$$k = AT^{\beta}\exp\left(-\frac{T_a}{T}\right) \quad (9.5)$$

title: Flux Evaluation
content_summary: This chapter explains that blastFoam uses explicit solutions for evolving conservative variables, which offers benefits in accuracy and computational cost. Higher-order, explicit Runge-Kutta time integration schemes improve time-integration accuracy. Stability is managed with SSP time integration methods or restrictive limiters. The calculation of conservative, hyperbolic fluxes relies on interpolated values on cell faces, found using a combination of a base interpolation scheme and flux limiters. MUSCL reconstruction is also implemented for more accurate face value reconstruction.
content: blastFoam relies on explicit solutions for the evolution of conservative variables. This has several benefits in terms of order of accuracy and computational cost. First, higher-order, explicit Runge-Kutta time integration schemes can be used which allow for higher order time-integration accuracy. Secondly, because of the required time steps of the problems of interest, iterative matrix solutions are not necessary to ensure accuracy or stability. Instead, stability can be improved through use of SSP time integraion methods or more restrictive limiters. All of the methods currently available to calculate the conservative, hyperbolic fluxes rely on the owner (own) and neighbour (nei) interpolated values on a face (also called left and right states). These interpolated values are found using a combination of a base interpolation scheme and flux limiters. OpenFOAM currently has a wide selection of available limiters such as upwind, Minmod, vanLeer, SuperBee, etc. for scalars, and upwind, MinmodV, vanLeerV, SuperBeeV, etc. for vectors; all are run-time selectable. In addition to the standard OpenFOAM interpolation schemes, MUSCL reconstruction has also been implemented. There are two main options which include upwindMUSCL and quadraticMUSCL. The upwind version uses standard upwinding for owner and neighbour interpolations. The quadratic version uses limiters in combination with the gradients and the gradient of the gradients (Hessians) to reconstruct face values. All of the standard limiters are available for MUSCL reconstruction. The vector limiters (MinmodV for example) are not used with the MUSCL schemes since the limiting is done for each component of the variables. An example of the fvSchemes dictionary is shown below

title: Riemann Solvers
content_summary: This section describes the available Riemann solvers. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u represents the normal velocity component. The solutions include the volume fraction for multi-fluid problems, but these details are omitted from the descriptions, except for the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases.
content: The currently available Riemann solvers are described below. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u = u · n. There is the inclusion of volume fraction in the solution of multi-fluid problems, however with the exception of the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases these changes will not be included in the descriptions below.

title: HLL
content_summary: The HLL scheme, based on Toro et al. (1994), neglects the contact wave. The fluxes are determined based on the wave propagation speeds, which are approximated from the owner and neighbor states using Roe averages.
content:
The HLL scheme is based on Toro et al. (1994) in which the contact wave is neglected. The fluxes take the form:

$$F^{HLL} = \begin{cases} F_{own} & \text{if } 0 \le S_{own} \\ \frac{S_{nei}F_{own} - S_{own}F_{nei} + S_{own}S_{nei}(U_{nei} - U_{own})}{S_{nei} - S_{own}} & \text{if } S_{own} \le 0 \le S_{nei} \\ F_{nei} & \text{if } 0 \ge S_{nei} \end{cases} \quad (10.1)$$

The owner and neighbor states to approximate the wave propagation speeds are defined as

$$S_{own} = \min(u_{own} - c_{own}, \tilde{u} - \tilde{c}) \quad (10.2)$$

$$S_{nei} = \max(u_{nei} + c_{nei}, \tilde{u} + \tilde{c}) \quad (10.3)$$

where uK is the normal flux of the respective state, cK is the speed of sound, and u˜ and c˜ are the Roe averages velocities and speed of sounds, respectively.

title: HLLC
content_summary: The HLLC Riemann solver, developed by Toro et al. (1994), improves on the HLL method by estimating the contact wave between the owner and neighbor waves. This implementation, from Zheng et al. (2011, 2008), results in two states between the waves, with constant pressure and velocity across the contact wave.
content:
The approximate HLLC Riemann was developed by Toro et al. (1994), and improves upon the HLL method by providing an estimation of the contact wave between the owner and neighbour waves. The specific implementation used is taken from Zheng et al. (2011, 2008) This means that the state between the owner and neighbour waves now has two states rather than one. The following fluxes are thus used, using the fact that both pressure and velocity are constant across the contact wave,

$$F^{HLLC} = \begin{cases} F_{own} & \text{if } 0 \le S_{own} \\ F^*_{own} & \text{if } S_{own} \le 0 \le S_* \\ F^*_{nei} & \text{if } S_* \le 0 \le S_{nei} \\ F_{nei} & \text{if } 0 \ge S_{nei} \end{cases} \quad (10.4)$$

The contact wave speed is given by

$$S_* = \frac{\rho_{own}u_{own}(S_{own} - U_{own}) + \rho_{nei}u_{nei}(S_{nei} - U_{nei}) + p_{nei} - p_{own}}{\rho_{own}(S_{own} - u_{own}) - \rho_{nei}(S_{nei} - u_{nei})} \quad (10.5)$$

and the pressure in the

$$p_{*K} = \rho_K(S_K - U_K)(S_* - u_K) + p_K \quad (10.6)$$

The final fluxes are determined by

$$F^{HLLC}_{*K} = \frac{S_*(S_K U_K - F_K) + S_K p_{*K} D_*}{S_K - S_*} \quad (10.7)$$

$$D_* = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ \mathbf{n} \\ S_* \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.8)$$

title: HLLCP
content_summary: Following the work of Xie et al. (2019), a low-speed correction has been added to the standard HLLC flux scheme. It is currently only available for single-phase simulations.
content:
Following the work of Xie et al. (2019), a low-speed correction of the standard HLLC flux scheme has been added. Currently it is only available for single phase simulations.

$$F^{HLLC-P} = F^{HLLC} + \Phi^P \quad (10.9)$$

$$\Phi_p = (f - 1) \frac{S_{own}S_{nei}}{S_{nei} - S_{own}} \frac{1}{1 + |\tilde{M}|} \frac{p_{nei} - p_{own}}{\tilde{c}^2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ \tilde{u} \\ \frac{1}{2}\tilde{u}^2 \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.10)$$

$$f = \min\left(\frac{p_{own}}{p_{nei}}, \frac{p_{nei}}{p_{own}}\right)^3 \quad (10.11)$$

where the minimum of all cell faces is used for each cell.

$$p_{**} = \theta p_* + (1 - \theta) \frac{p_{own} + p_{nei}}{2} \quad (10.12)$$

$$\theta = \min(M_*, 1) \quad (10.13)$$

$$p_{***} = f p_{**} + (1 - f)p_* \quad (10.14)$$

where p*** replaces p*K in Eq. (10.7)

title: AUSM+
content_summary: The AUSM+ scheme, developed by Luo et al. (2004), constructs fluxes based on the mass flux across the face. The star state variables are written as functions of the owner and neighbor states.
content:
The AUSM+ scheme was developed by Luo et al. (2004) and constructs the fluxes based on the mass flux across the face.

$$F^{AUSM+} = 0.5 [M_*c_*(U_{own} + U_{nei})] - 0.5 [|M_*|c_*(U_{own} + U_{nei})] + F_p \quad (10.15)$$

$$F_p = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ p_*\mathbf{n} \\ p_* \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.16)$$

The star state variables are written as

$$c_* = 0.5(c_{own} + c_{nei}) \quad (10.17)$$

$$M_* = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} \quad (10.18)$$

and

$$P_* = P^+_{5,own}p_{own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{nei} \quad (10.19)$$

where

$$M^{\pm}_1(M) = 0.5(M \pm |M|) \quad (10.20)$$

$$M^{\pm}_2(M) = \begin{cases} M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ \pm 0.25(M \pm 1)^2 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.21)$$

$$M^{\pm}_4(M) = \begin{cases} M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ M^{\pm}_2(M)[1 \mp 16\beta M^{\mp}_2(M)] & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.22)$$

$$P^{\pm}_5(M) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{M}M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ \pm M^{\pm}_2(M)[(2 \mp M) - 16\alpha M M^{\mp}_2(M)] & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.23)$$

with α = 3/16 and β = 1/8.

title: AUSM+up
content_summary: The AUSM+up scheme extends the AUSM+ scheme with a low-speed correction by Liou (2006). It uses the high-speed portion from the previous section but adds diffusion terms, resulting in new definitions for the mass and pressure fluxes. This correction is important for blast simulations where Mach numbers can become much less than unity, which can lead to numerical checker-boarding.
content:
The AUSM+ scheme extended to include a low speed correction by Liou (2006). The previous section is used to describe the high speed portion, however additional diffusion terms are added resulting in the following definitions of the mass and pressure fluxes

$$M_* = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} + M_p \quad (10.24)$$

and

$$P_* = P^+_{5,own}p_{own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{nei} + P_u \quad (10.25)$$

where

$$M_p = -\frac{K_p}{f_a}\max(1 - \sigma \bar{M}a^2, 0) \frac{p_{nei} - p_{own}}{\rho_* c_*^2} \quad (10.26)$$

$$P_u = -K_u P^+_{5,own} P^-_{5,nei} (\rho_{own} + \rho_{nei}) f_a c_* (u_{nei} - u_{own}) \quad (10.27)$$

and fa = 1, σ = 1, Kp = 0.25, Ku = 0.75, and α is redefined as 3/16(5fa − 4). The interface density, ρ*, is defined as (ρown+ρnei)/2, and the average Mach number is defined as.

$$\bar{M}a^2 = \frac{u_{own}^2 + u_{nei}^2}{2c_*^2} \quad (10.28)$$

This correction is very important when considering blasts because the Mach numbers can become much less than unity, resulting in numerical checker-boarding.

title: AUSM+up (granular)
content_summary: A modified version of the AUSM+up flux scheme, developed by Houim and Oran (2016), includes flux limiters for packing events. It defines the flux of conservative variables (mass, momentum, thermal energy, and granular energy) across a face with specific formulas.
content:
A modified version of the AUSM+up flux scheme has been developed by Houim and Oran (2016) and includes flux limiters in the event of packing. The flux of conservative variables, (mass, momentum, thermal energy, and granular energy) across a face is given by

$$F^{AUSM+up}_s = F_p + \dot{m} \begin{cases} U_{own} & \text{if } \dot{m} > 0 \\ U_{nei} & \text{if } \dot{m} \le 0 \end{cases} \quad (10.29)$$

where

$$\dot{m} = F c_{1/2} M_{1/2} \begin{cases} \alpha_{own}\rho_{own} & \text{if } M_{1/2} > 0 \\ \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei} & \text{if } M_{1/2} \le 0 \end{cases} \quad (10.30)$$

$$F = \frac{(c_{1/2} - \epsilon)[1 + |M_{1/2}|(1 - G/2)]}{2} \frac{\max(\alpha_{s,own}, \alpha_{s,nei})}{\alpha_{s,max}} [\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} - \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}] \quad (10.31)$$

and Fp = (0, Ptot,1/2I, 0, 0), with αs being the total granular volume fraction (sum of all granular phases), αs,max being the maximum volume fraction at which packing occurs, and epsilon being a small number (1e-10). G is defined as

$$G = \max(2(1 - D\zeta^2)) \quad (10.32)$$

and ζ is defined as

$$\zeta = \begin{cases} \frac{\alpha_s^M - \alpha_{s,crit}}{\alpha_{s,max} - \alpha_{s,crit}} & \text{if } \alpha_s^M > \alpha_{s,crit} \\ 0 & \text{if } \alpha_s^M < \alpha_{s,crit} \end{cases} \quad (10.33)$$

where αMs is the maximum granular volume fraction of the own/nei value, and αs,crit being the minimum granular volume fraction that frictional stresses occur. c1/2 is defined as

$$c_{1/2} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha_{own}\rho_{own}c_{own}^2 + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}c_{nei}^2}{\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}} + \epsilon} \quad (10.34)$$

The split Mach number is defined as

$$M_{1/2} = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} - 2\frac{K_p}{f_a}\max(1 - \sigma \bar{M}^2, 0) \frac{P_{tot,nei-}, P_{tot,own}}{(\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei} + \epsilon)c_{1/2}^2} \quad (10.35)$$

where M±4 is calculate the same way as in the AUSM+ scheme and fa = 1. Kp, and σ are defined as

$$K_p = 0.25 + 0.75(1 - G/2) \quad (10.36)$$

$$\sigma = 0.75\sigma/2 \quad (10.37)$$

The pressure is defined as

$$P_{tot,1/2} = -K_u f_a(c_{1/2} - \epsilon)P^+_{5,own}P^-_{5,nei}(\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei})(u_{nei} - u_{own}) + P^+_{5,own}p_{tot,own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{tot,nei} \quad (10.38)$$

with P±5 being the same as in the AUSM+ scheme and

$$K_u = 0.75 + 0.25(1 - G/2) \quad (10.39)$$

title: Tadmor/Kurganov
content_summary: The Tadmor/Kurganov fluxes were developed by Kurganov and Tadmor (2000) and are utilized by the rhoCentalFoam solver (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018a; Greenshields et al., 2010), which has been ported to the current framework. Two run-time selectable options (Kurganov or Tadmor) are available, following the same general procedure with a slight difference in coefficient calculation.
content:
The Tadmor/Kurganov fluxes were developed by Kurganov and Tadmor (2000). The rhoCentalFoam solver (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018a; Greenshields et al., 2010) utilizes this scheme, which has been ported to the current framework. Two run-time selectable options (e.g. Kurganov or Tadmor) are available; both follow the same general procedure with a slight difference in the calculation of coefficients. The Kurganov scheme defines

$$a^+ = \max(\max(u_{own} + c_{own}, u_{nei} + c_{nei}), 0) \quad (10.40)$$

$$a^- = \min(\min(u_{own} - c_{own}, u_{nei} - c_{nei}), 0) \quad (10.41)$$

$$a_{own} = \frac{a^+}{a^+ + a^-} \quad (10.42)$$

$$a_{nei} = \frac{a^-}{a^+ + a^-} \quad (10.43)$$

and

$$a = \frac{a^- a^+}{a^+ + a^-} \quad (10.44)$$

The Tadmor scheme defines aown = anei = 0.5 and a = max(|a−|, |a+|). The volumetric fluxes are written

$$\phi_{own} = u_{own}a_{own} - a \quad (10.45)$$

and

$$\phi_{nei} = u_{nei}a_{nei} + a \quad (10.46)$$

The resulting fluxes are

$$F^{KT} = (\phi_{own}U_{own} + \phi_{nei}U_{nei}) + F_p \quad (10.47)$$

with

$$F_p = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ (a_{own}p_{own} + a_{nei}p_{nei})\mathbf{n} \\ a(p_{own} - p_{nei}) \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.48)$$

title: Riemann advection scheme
content_summary: This section describes an additional advection scheme using Riemann fluxes, which has been added for consistency between conserved quantities and other transport quantities like turbulence fields or species mass fractions. This allows the use of higher-order interpolation schemes for additional scalar transport, a major advantage over traditional methods that are often limited to upwinding in the presence of strong gradients. However, one limitation is that with very stiff implicit terms (e.g., in turbulence models), the explicit advection term can become negligible, leading to no advection. Therefore, implicit divergence schemes are still recommended for turbulence quantities with large implicit source terms.
content:
In order to allow for consistency between the conserved quantities (mass, momentum, and energy), and additional transport quantities such as turbulence fields or species mass fractions, an additional advection scheme using the Riemann fluxes has been added. This makes use of the quantities calculated in transport of the conserved variable which then allows for the use of higher order interpolation schemes to be used for additional scalar transport. This is a major advantage over traditional methods which are generally limited to upwinding for additional transport due to presence of strong gradients.

NOTE: One limitation of using the Riemann fluxes is that, because an explicit advection term for the transported quantities is used, when very stiff implicit terms are present (such as production term in turbulence models), the advection term can become negligible leading to no advection occurring. For this reason, implicit divergence schemes are still recommended for the advection of turbulence quantities and other equations with large implicit source terms.

title: Time integration
content_summary: This section explains that higher-order time integration has been added, allowing for fully third-order accurate solutions, a significant improvement over standard OpenFOAM's at-most second-order implicit time evolution. This applies to both conservative quantities and activation and afterburn models. The transport of turbulent quantities is still limited by standard time integration schemes. The available schemes and their mathematical steps are detailed, with the scheme specified in the fvSchemes dictionary. Strong stability preserving (SSP) methods offer variations with more steps for added stability.
content:
Higher order time integration has been added allowing for fully third order accurate solutions to the evolution equations. This is a major benefit in comparison to standard OpenFOAM time integration which is at most second order due to the limitation on the implicit time evolution. This functionality applies to both conservative quantities such as mass, momentum, and energy, as well as the activation and afterburn models. The transport of turbulent quantities are still limited by the standard time integration schemes in OpenFOAM. Below are the currently available time integration schemes and the mathematical steps associated with them. The superscript n denotes the state at the beginning of the current time step, (i) denotes the i-th step, and n + 1 denotes final state. The time integration scheme is specified in the fvSchemes dictionary using

NOTE: The strong stability preserving (SSP) methods offer several different variation that allow for a greater number of steps to be used to add additional stability to the solution. This is specified by adding the number of requested steps after the SSP scheme. If a number is provided that is higher or lower than the available number of steps, the scheme will used the closest number of steps.

title: Euler
content_summary: Describes the standard first-order explicit Euler time integration method.
content:
Standard first order time integration

$$ U^{n+1} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.49) $$

title: RK1SSP
content_summary: This section outlines the first-order, strong-stability-preserving (SSP) Runge-Kutta method, based on the work of Spiteri and Ruuth (2002). It details the formulas for one-stage, two-stage, and three-stage implementations of the method.
content:
First-order, strong-stability-preserving Runge-Kutta method (Spiteri and Ruuth, 2002):
One stage:
$$ U^{n+1} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.50) $$
Two stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.51) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = \frac{1}{2} U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.52) $$
Three stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.53) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.54) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = U^{(2)} - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(2)} \quad (10.55) $$

title: RK2
content_summary: This section describes the standard second-order Runge-Kutta method, also known as the midpoint method.
content:
Standard second-order Runge-Kutta method (mid point):
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.56) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.57) $$

title: RK2SSP
content_summary: Details the second-order, strong-stability-preserving (SSP) Runge-Kutta method from Spiteri and Ruuth (2002). Formulas are provided for two-stage, three-stage and four-stage implementations.
content:
Second-order, strong-stability-preserving Runge-Kutta method (Spiteri and Ruuth, 2002):
Two stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.58) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = \frac{1}{2} (U^n + U^{(1)} - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)}) \quad (10.59) $$
Three stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.60) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.61) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = \frac{1}{3} U^n + \frac{2}{3} U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(2)} \quad (10.62) $$
Four stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.63) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.64) $$
$$ U^{(3)} = U^{(2)} - \frac{1}{3} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(2)} \quad (10.65) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = \frac{1}{4} U^n + \frac{3}{4} U^{(3)} - \frac{1}{4} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(3)} \quad (10.66) $$

title: RK3SSP
content_summary: Presents the third-order, strong-stability-preserving (SSP) Runge-Kutta method from Spiteri and Ruuth (2002). Formulas are provided for both three-stage and four-stage implementations.
content:
Third-order, strong-stability-preserving Runge-Kutta method (Spiteri and Ruuth, 2002):
Three stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.67) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4} (3U^n + U^{(1)} - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)}) \quad (10.68) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = \frac{1}{3} (U^n + 2U^{(2)} - 2\Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)}) \quad (10.69) $$
Four stages:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.70) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.71) $$
$$ U^{(3)} = \frac{2}{3} U^n + \frac{1}{3} U^{(2)} - \frac{1}{6} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(2)} \quad (10.72) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = U^{(3)} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(3)} \quad (10.73) $$

title: RK4
content_summary: Provides the formulas for the standard fourth-order Runge-Kutta method.
content:
Standard fourth-order Runge-Kutta method:
$$ U^{(1)} = U^n - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^n \quad (10.74) $$
$$ U^{(2)} = U^{(1)} - \frac{1}{2} \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(1)} \quad (10.75) $$
$$ U^{(3)} = U^n - \Delta t \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(2)} \quad (10.76) $$
$$ U^{n+1} = U^n - \frac{1}{6} \Delta t \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{F}^n + 2\mathbf{F}^{(1)} + 2\mathbf{F}^{(2)} + \mathbf{F}^{(3)}) \quad (10.77) $$

title: RK4SSP
content_summary: This section details the fourth-order, strong-stability-preserving (SSP) Runge-Kutta method developed by Spiteri and Ruuth (2002). The general formula for the iterative update is presented, which depends on coefficients provided in two separate tables.
content:
Fourth-order, strong-stability-preserving Runge-Kutta method (Spiteri and Ruuth, 2002):
$$ U^{(i)} = \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (a_{ik} U^{(k)} + \Delta t \beta_{ik} \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}^{(k)}) \quad (10.78) $$

title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR)
content_summary: This chapter describes the integration of dynamic mesh classes for Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR), based on the work of Rettenmaier et al. (2019). The library supports 2-D, 2-D axisymmetric, and 3-D meshes with dynamic load balancing. The original 'adaptiveFvMesh' class has been enhanced for compressible flows, improving stability with meshes from 'snappyHexMesh'. Additionally, polyhedral refinement capabilities have been ported from foam-extend. A new feature allows delaying the start of unrefinement, which is useful for simulations with delayed detonations. A 'movingAdaptiveFvMesh' class is also available, which combines AMR with mesh motion, recommended for use with the 'velocityLaplacian' motion solver. Important notes are provided regarding limitations when using snappyHexMesh for 2-D or axisymmetric refinement and the potential for crashes with 'snapped' meshes.
content:
The dynamic mesh classes have been incorporated from the github repository SOFTX_2018_143 and is based on the work of Rettenmaier et al. (2019). This library introduces 2-D, 2-D axisymmetic, and 3-D (present in OpenFOAM-9) all with the option to include dynamic load balancing. The original work (dynamicRefineBalanceFvMesh and dynamicRefineFvMesh) has been used as the base for the adaptiveFvMesh class. This mesh type has been further developed, specifically for compressible flows, and includes modifications for improved stability with meshes created with snappyHexMesh. Additional polyhedral refinement has been ported from foam-extend’s and allows for general polygon refinement.
Because the detonation of explosives may not occur when the simulation starts, the option to begin unrefinement at a designated time (”beginUnrefine”) has been added. This allows the mesh to remain refined in specific regions even though the error is initially small.
Additionally, the movingAdaptiveFvMesh class is based on adaptiveFvMesh, and has all of the same features, but includes the ability to use mesh motion. It is recommended to use the velocityLaplacian motion solver.
NOTE: Whilst AMR may be used with meshes created using snappy-HexMesh, regions can only be cut at level 0 when using either 2-D or axisymmetric refinement. This is due to the fact that snappyHexMesh uses a 3-D refinement, regardless of the number of solution directions. If refinement is used, the simulation will crash when the mesh is refined in these areas.
NOTE: Meshes that have been "snapped" with snappyHexMesh may work, however crashes may occur. Use at your own risk.

Write the cell and point level as viewable fields (false by default)

title: Error estimators
content_summary: This section explains how error estimators are used to determine which cells to refine or unrefine. The process generally involves evaluating a derivative or difference of a specified field across the faces of a cell and its neighbors. The maximum face error for a cell is then used as the cell-centered value to decide on refinement. Probe locations are automatically refined to the maximum level to maintain accuracy and avoid discontinuities in sampled data.
content: In order to determine the cells that can be refined or unrefined, error estimators have been added. Generally, this is achieved by looking for a derivative or difference of a specified fields across all the faces a cell and it neighbours. The maximum value of all face errors of a given cell is taken as the cell centerbased value, and is used to define the cells that should be refined. Probe locations are automatically refined up to the maximum level of refinement to remove discontinuities in probe sampling.

table.Description: Error that mesh is refined at
Error that refinement is stopped (default is great)
Error that unrefinement begins to occur again (default is great)

title: cellSize
content_summary: Refinement is determined based on the size of the cell. Users can specify criteria based on the cell's volume, the magnitude of its dimensions, its components (x, y, z), or a characteristic length. Since refinement is based on cell size, a large number of maximum refinement levels can be used.
content: Refinement is determined based on the cell size. Either the volume, magnitude of relevant directions (mag), components (cmpt), or the characteristic length (characteristic) can be used. Only the options listed below are used. Because the refinment is based on the cell size the maximumRefinment can be a large number.

table.Description: size type (volume, mag, cmpt, or characteristic)
Components (x, y, or z for cmpts)
Minimum size as a vector (for cmpts)
Maximum size as a vector (for cmpts)
Maximum number of refinement levels (default is 10)

title: fieldValue
content_summary: This error estimation method sets the error value directly to the value of a user-specified scalar field within the simulation.
content:
The error is set to the value of the given field
$$ \epsilon_x = x \quad (11.1) $$
where x is a field chosen by the user, and can be any scalar field stored within the simulation.

title: delta
content_summary: This is a basic method for detecting sharp changes across cell faces. The error is defined as the absolute difference between the values of a user-specified scalar field at the current face ('own') and the neighboring face ('nei').
content:
A basic method to detect sharp changes across faces uses a difference approach, where the error is defined by
$$ \epsilon_\delta = |x_{own} - x_{nei}| \quad (11.2) $$
where x is a field chosen by the user, and can be any scalar field stored within the simulation. The subscript own denotes values at the current face, while nei denotes the value at the neighboring face.

title: scaledDelta
content_summary: This method detects sharp changes using a scaled difference approach. The error is calculated by dividing the absolute difference between field values at the current and neighboring faces by a scaling factor. This factor is the maximum of a user-specified minimum value and the minimum distance of the two face values from a central offset value, which helps to normalize the error.
content:
A basic method to detect sharp changes across faces uses a scaled difference approach, where the error is defined by
$$ \bar{\epsilon_\delta} = \frac{|x_{own} - x_{nei}|}{\max(\min(|x_{own} - x_c|, |x_{nei} - x_c|), x_{min})} \quad (11.3) $$
where x is a field chosen by the user, and can be any field stored within the simulation. The minimum value (xmin) can be specified by the user and is the minimum value that the error will be scaled by.

table.Description: Minimum value to scale difference by (default is small)
Offset value to centre error around (default is 0)

title: Density gradient (densityGradient)
content_summary: This section describes a modified density gradient error estimator, based on the work of Zheng et al. (2008). The error is defined by an equation that takes the maximum of two terms, considering both the 'own' and 'nei' cell faces, to capture the gradient accurately.
content:
A modified density gradient was defined by Zheng et al. (2008), and uses an error defined by
$$ \epsilon_{
ablaho} = \max \left(  rac{|(
abla \ln ho)_C - (
abla \ln ho)_{own}|}{\alpha_f ho_C/dl + |
abla \ln ho)_{own}|},  rac{|(
abla \ln ho)_C - (
abla \ln ho)_{nei}|}{\alpha_f ho_C/dl + |
abla \ln ho)_{nei}|} ight) f \quad (11.4) $$
with
$$ (
abla \ln ho)_C =  rac{ho_{nei} - ho_{own}}{|\mathbf{r}_{nei} - \mathbf{r}_{own}|} \quad (11.5) $$
$$ (
abla \ln ho)_{nei,own} = (
abla ho)_{nei,own}  rac{\mathbf{r}_{nei} - \mathbf{r}_{own}}{|\mathbf{r}_{nei} - \mathbf{r}_{own}|} \quad (11.6) $$
rown,nei is the surface normal vector, and dl = |rnei − rown|, is the distance between cell centers. This error estimator uses the total density.

title: Lohner
content_summary: This section presents an error estimation method based on Löhner (1987). It employs a scaled Laplacian to produce an error value bounded between 0 and 1, indicating the smoothness of the solution. Originally designed for Cartesian grids, the method has been adapted for non-Cartesian grids by using cubic interpolation to determine the face value of the tracked field, which allows for non-zero smoothness across faces.
content:
A final error estimation method based on Löhner (1987) is also available. It uses a scaled Laplacian to ensure an error bounded between 0 and 1, and indicates the smoothness of the solution. Due to the fact that the method was initially developed for a Cartesian grid, some modifications were required. Namely, the face value of the tracked field is interpolated using a cubic interpolation method. This allows non-zero smoothness across the faces while still allowing for the method to be used on a non-Cartesian grid.
$$ \epsilon_{Lohner} = \frac{|x_{own} - 2x_f + x_{nei}|}{|x_{own} - x_f| + |x_{nei} - x_f| + \epsilon(|x_{own}| + 2|x_f| + |x_{nei}|)} \quad (11.7) $$
where ε is a coefficient which is problem dependent, and x is any scalar field saved within the simulation.

table.Description: Field name used to compute error

title: multiComponent
content_summary: The multicomponent error estimator allows for the use of multiple error criteria to determine cell refinement. Any of the previously described methods can be combined. A list of estimator names must be provided, each corresponding to a dictionary with its specific settings. The refinement decision for each cell is based on the maximum error value calculated from all the specified estimators. Each estimator can also have its own maximum refinement level.

title: Dynamic Load Balancing
content_summary: This section discusses the availability of dynamic load balancing for 2-D, 2-D axisymmetric, and 3-D meshes, which can significantly reduce computational time by distributing cells evenly across processors. However, it also highlights known limitations. Large cases may exceed the MPI buffer limit during data transfer, causing crashes. Additionally, issues can arise when transferring non-scalar fields and with 'movingWall' boundary conditions. While these problems have been addressed for the standard 'blastFoam' solver, stability is not guaranteed if 'adaptiveFvMesh' is used with a different solver.
content: The ability to use dynamic load balancing is available for 2-D, 2-D axisymmetric, and 3-D meshes. Dynamic load balancing can greatly reduce the computational time required by balancing the cells equally across all processors. However, there are known limitations: due to the way OpenFOAM transfers data between processors, the MPI buffer limit can be exceeded for large cases, leading to the simulation crashing. There are also known problems in the transferring of data for calculated non-scalar fields and movingWall boundary conditions. The fields used within the standard blastFoam solver have addressed this, but if adaptiveFvMesh is used with a different solver, its stability with balancing is not guaranteed.

table.Description: List of error estimators to be used

title: Example
content_summary: This section provides an example of the optional 'dynamicMeshDict' file used with the 'adaptiveFvMesh' class.

title: Numerics
content_summary: This chapter presents a collection of numerical methods used within the previously described models, which can also be applied to new applications and solvers. The reader is directed to the provided references for more comprehensive descriptions of the methods.
content: A collection of numerical methods is provided that are used within the models describe prior, and can also be used for new application and solvers. Many of the methods are only listed and the reader should view the provided references for a more complete description.

title: Lookup tables
content_summary: This section describes the availability of 1D, 2D, and 3D lookup tables. These tables support modified data types, runtime-selectable interpolation schemes, and can read or construct data. The dictionary names and inputs are model-specific, using 'x' and 'f' as placeholders. If a dictionary named 'xCoeffs' is used, the 'x' prefix is dropped for other inputs (e.g., 'xMod' becomes 'mod'). For 1D data, the field can be constructed in three ways: read directly, read from a specific column in a file, or generated using a specified spacing.
content:
1D, 2D, and 3D lookup tables are provided, all including support for modified data types, runtime selectable interpolation schemes, and the ability to read or construct the necessary data. The name of the given dictionaries and inputs are dictated by the specific models using the tables, "x" and "f" will be used as place holders for these names. If the dictionary named ”xCoeffs” is provided ”x” is not used to read the given input (i.e. ”xMod” is ”mod”).
When 1D data is read the field can be constructed with 3 methods: directly read, read from a file given a column, or constructed with a spacing.

table.Description: Name of a file to read from
Delimiter used to seperate data (’,’ by default)
Column of the data (first column by default)

title: Modifiers
content_summary: This section lists the different modifiers available for interpolating scalar data in various mathematical spaces. The options include 'none' (no modification), 'log10', 'pow10' (10^x), 'ln' (natural logarithm), and 'exp' (exponential).
content:
Different modifiers can be used to interpolate scalar data in different spaces, and include
- none
- log10
- pow10 (10^x)
- ln
- exp

title: Interpolation schemes
content_summary: This section outlines several interpolation schemes that give users control over how data is interpolated. The available methods include 'floor', 'ceil', and various 'Clamp' and 'Extrapolated' versions for linear, quadratic, and cubic interpolation. 'Clamp' methods restrict the interpolated values to the minimum and maximum of the provided table, while 'Extrapolated' methods use the nearest values to extend the data beyond the table's bounds. The 'interpolationScheme' keyword is used to set the default method for all directions.
content:
Several interpolation schemes are provided to give the user control over the method which is used to interpolate data including
- floor
- ceil
- linearClamp
- linearExtrapolated
- quadraticClamp
- quadraticExtrapolted
- cubicClamp
- cubicExtrapolated
Clamp methods limit the interpolation to the minimum and maximum values of the provided table, and extrapolated methods will use the closest values to extrapolate the given data out of bounds. Different methods can be used for all directions, and ”interpolationScheme” is used to define the default method.

title: 1D lookup tables
content_summary: Data is retrieved based on a single input value 'x'. The implementation supports calculating first and second derivatives. Additionally, a reverse lookup feature is available for scalar data, allowing one to find the input 'x' that corresponds to a given function value 'f'.
content: Data is looked up given an x value. First and second derivatives have been implemented and a reverse lookup can be used for scalar data (find f given x).

title: 2D lookup table
content_summary: This section describes 2D lookup tables where data is retrieved based on 'x' and 'y' values. Data can be read from a table or a list, with a comma as the default delimiter. The table can be flipped (transposed) during reading if the input is specified as y-by-x. The implementation includes first and second derivatives (both pure and mixed) and supports reverse lookup for scalar data (e.g., finding 'x' given 'f' and 'y').
content: Data is looked up given x and y values. The data can be read from a table or from a list. The default delimiter is ",". The table can also be flipped when reading if the input is reversed (y by x) and a check is done on the size when reading. First and second derivatives (pure and mixed) have been implemented and a reverse lookup can be used for scalar data (find x or y given f and y or x).

title: 3D lookup tables
content_summary: This section covers 3D lookup tables, where data is retrieved using x, y, and z values. Data can be read from a table or a list, with default delimiters of ',' for columns and ';' for rows. The table can be flipped if the input order is reversed. The implementation includes first and second derivatives (both pure and mixed).
content: Data is looked up given x, y, and z values. The data can be read from a table or a list. The default delimiters are "," for columns and ";" for rows. The table can also be flipped when reading if the input is reversed (z by y by x) and a check is done on the size when reading. First and second derivatives (pure and mixed) have been implemented.

title: Equations
content_summary: A simple equation class has been implemented to support various numerical methods. It takes an input and returns a result. This class can function as either a univariate equation, where a list of scalar inputs returns a single value, or a multivariate equation, where a list of scalar inputs returns a list of values.
content:
A simple equation class has been implemented to support many of the numerical methods. The equations take and input and return a result.
Equation: A single scalar input is used to return a single value. This can be used as a univariate or multivariate equation.
Univariate equation: A List of scalar inputs are used to return a single value. This can be used as a multivariate equation.
Multivariate equation: A list of scalar inputs are used to return a list of values.

title: Root solvers
content_summary: This section lists a collection of root-finding solvers for both univariate and multivariate scalar equations. For univariate solvers, convergence is checked based on both absolute and relative tolerances for the independent variable (x) and an absolute tolerance for the function value (y). The available univariate methods include bisection, Brent's method, false position, Halley's method, Newton-Raphson, secant, Steffensen's method, and a simple step method. For multivariate problems, the available solvers are goodBroyden, badBroyden, and Newton-Raphson.
content:
A collection of root solvers are provided for finding the roots of univariate, scalar equations. Both the x (absolute and relative) and y (absolute only) values are checked for convergence. The available methods are:
Univariate root solvers:
- bisection
- Brent
- falsePoint
- Halley
- NewtonRaphson
- secant
- Steffensen
- step
Multivariate root solvers:
- goodBroyden (Broyden, 1965)
- badBroyden (Kvaalen, 1991)
- NewtonRaphson

title: Minimization
content_summary: This section presents a collection of minimization methods for finding the minima of scalar equations, implemented based on the work of Kochenderfer and Wheeler (2019). For univariate minimization, the available models are bisection, Fibonacci, golden ratio, Newton-Raphson, quadratic fit, Shubert-Piyavskii, and a simple step method. For multivariate minimization, the options are gradient descent, Nelder-Mead, and particle swarm. Specific options are provided for configuring the Nelder-Mead and Particle Swarm algorithms, as well as general options for convergence criteria.
content:
A collection of minimization methods are providing for finding the minimums of scalar equations. All methods were implemented using Kochenderfer and Wheeler (2019). The available models are
Univariate minimization:
- bisection
- Fibonacci
- goldenRatio
- NewtonRaphson
- quadraticFit
- ShubertPiyavskii
- step
Multivariate minimization:
- gradientDescent
- NelderMead
- particleSwarm

Absolute convergence tolerance for the evaluated function (1e-6 by default)

title: Integrators
content_summary: This section describes numerical integrators implemented for arbitrary data types, not just scalars. Univariate integrators, which take a single input, can use either adaptive integration or a fixed number of intervals and are optimized to reduce function evaluations. The available univariate methods include Boole's rule, Gaussian quadrature, midpoint rule, Simpson's 1/3 rule, Simpson's 3/8 rule, and the trapezoidal rule. For multivariate integration, a smaller subset of these methods (Gaussian, midpoint, trapezoidal) is available due to the complexity of higher dimensions, and they lack the same level of optimization as the univariate versions.
content:
Numerical integrators have been implemented for arbitrary data types (not only scalars).
Univariate integrators (single input) have the ability to use either adaptive integration or a fixed number of intervals. These methods have also been optimised to reduce the number of function evaluations. The available methods are:
Univariate integrators:
- Boole
- Gaussian
- midPoint
- Simpson13
- Simpson38
- trapezoidal
Because of the complexity of integrating an arbitrary number of dimensions, only a small subset of the univariate integrators a have been implemented for multiple inputs. These methods also do not have the same optimisation as seen in the univariate case. The available options are:
Multivariate integrators:
- Gaussian
- midPoint
- trapezoidal
