Is the integration adaptive (true by default)

title: Solid models
content_summary: This chapter describes the integration of solid mechanics models from the 'solids4foam' library into the 'blastFSI' solver. These models enable the simulation of both linear and non-linear deformation using a finite-volume methodology, as detailed in works by Cardiff et al. and Simo and Hughes. If 'solids4foam' is available locally, the 'blastSolids4Foam' library can be compiled to allow the 'solids4Foam' solver to use the fluid models available in 'blastFoam'. It is noted that these models are still under development and have not been extensively validated for blast applications.
content:
A port of solids4foam has been incorporated into the blastFSI solver. These models allow for linear and non-linear deformation using the finite-volume methodolgy described in Cardiff et al. (2018); Cardiff and Demirdzic (2021) and Simo and Hughes (2000) for plasticity.
In addition, if solids4foam is available locally, the blastSolids4Foam library can also be compiled to allow use of the models available in the blastFoam solver to be used with solids4Foam solver by specifying the blast fluid.
NOTE: These models are still under development and have not been extensively validated specifically for blast.

title: Coupling to preCICE’s OpenFOAM adapter
content_summary: This chapter explains the capability of coupling blastFoam with an external solver through the preCICE library. This feature is enabled by the addition of moving mesh functionality and is ready to use upon installation without special setup for standard moving mesh classes. However, when using the 'movingAdaptiveFvMesh', there are limitations: the coupled boundary cannot change during the simulation, which means load balancing is not allowed, and the 'protectedPatches' keyword must be used to specify boundaries that cannot be refined. Additionally, checkpointing cannot be used with refinement due to mesh-related limitations.
content: With the addition of moving meshes, the ability to couple blastFoam to an external solver through preCICE is possible. There is no special setup required, and it is ready to be used upon install. There are no limitations for the standard blastFoam moving mesh classes. However, when using the movingAdaptiveFvMesh, the coupled boundary cannot change within the simulation. For this reason, balancing is not allowed and the optional ”protectedPatches” keyword must be used to specify boundaries which cannot be refined. Checkpointing can also not be used with refinement due to limitations on the mesh.

title: Function objects
content_summary: This chapter introduces additional blast-specific function objects that supplement the standard OpenFOAM function objects. While standard sampling library objects are included by default, other libraries can be added as well. An example is provided showing how to include these additional libraries in the 'controlDict' file.

title: blastProbes
content_summary: This section describes 'blastProbes,' a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM probe utility. It adds several key features: the ability to append data to existing probe files, allowing for a single file across simulation restarts; the option to move probes that are outside the mesh to the nearest face to obtain valid results, which is useful for curved surfaces; and the removal of warnings about probes existing on multiple processors by selecting a single processor for probing. A note of caution is added: during mesh refinement and unrefinement, probe locations can 'jump' to the nearest cell, causing fluctuations in plotted data. This can be mitigated by enabling probe refinement in the 'dynamicMeshDict' file.
content:
A modified version of the standard OpenFOAM probe utility is also available, where the basic functionality remains the same, with several key additions. First, the ability to append to exiting probe files is now possible, allowing for a single probe file to be used even if a simulation is restarted from a later time. Second, probes that are not found within the mesh can be moved to the nearest face so they can produce valid results. This can be very useful if a probe is needed on a curved face. Lastly, the warning of probes existing on multiple processors has been removed by selecting a single processor on which to probe.
NOTE: When refinement and unrefinement occurs, the probe locations will "jump" to the nearest cell resulting in fluctuations when plotted. This can be eliminated by allowing refinement of probes in dynamicMeshDict (”refineProbes”) (this is the default option).

title: fieldMinMax (fieldMax)
content_summary: This function object outputs the minimum or maximum value of any specified field at every cell in the domain. This is useful for visualizing quantities like maximum impulse or peak pressure. These min/max fields are automatically saved during the calculation.
content: The minimum or maximum value at every cell in a domain is output, allowing for quantities such as maximum impulse and peak pressure to be visualized. This can be done for any field currently stored within the simulation. These fields are automatically saved within the calculation.

title: writeTimeList
content_summary: This utility allows for writing output fields at a user-specified list of times, which is useful when data is needed at times that do not align with the standard write interval.
content: Additional write times are added using a list of user specified times. This is useful when fields are needed at time that do not correspond to a standard write interval. See validation/blastFoam/woodwardColella for an example.

title: conservedQuantities
content_summary: This function object computes and prints the volume-integrated value of specified fields. It outputs the initial, current, total difference, and relative difference for each tracked field. A note indicates that the removal and injection of values into a domain are not currently considered.
content:
The volume integrated value of fields can be computed and printed in the output of the solver. The initial, current, total difference, and relative difference is printed. Any fields can be tracked by specifying the names of the fields in the dictionary.
NOTE: Currently, the removal and injection of values into a domain is not considered.

title: vtkTimeSeries surface writer
content_summary: This is an extension of the standard VTK surface writer. It creates an additional '*.vtk.series' file in the post-processing directory, which contains relative paths to the surface data at all write times. This file can be opened in ParaView to easily visualize the time sequence of the surface data.
content: An extension of the vtk surface writer for writing sampled surfaces can be used so that an additional *.vtk.series file is written in the postProcessing/-surfacesName folder with the relative paths to all write times of the given surfaces. This file can then be opened in ParaView to visualize all files in the correct time sequence.

title: blastMachNo
content_summary: This function object replaces the standard 'machNo' utility in OpenFOAM. It calculates the Mach number using the speed of sound derived from the specific thermodynamic models available in blastFoam.
content: The blastMachNo replaces the standard machNo available in OpenFOAM by using the speed of sound calculated from the blastFoam specific thermodynamic models.

table.Description: Name of the phase (default is none)
Name of the velocity field (default is U or U.phaseName)
Name of the Mach number field (default is Ma or Ma.phaseName)

title: dynamicPressure
content_summary: This utility is useful for tracking the dynamic pressure (kinetic energy) of a phase in blast simulations. It returns the vector of dynamic pressure, the magnitude of which is the standard dynamic pressure. For calculating static or total pressure, the standard OpenFOAM 'pressure' function object can be used.
content:
It can be useful in blast simulations to track the dynamic pressure (kinetic energy) of a phase defined as
$$ p_{dyn} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_i U |U| \quad (15.1) $$
The dynamicPressure function object returns the vector of dynamic pressure, where the magnitude of this output is the standard dynamic pressure. Additionally the pressure function object (standard OpenFOAM) can be used to calculate static (if using incompressible) or total pressure and their respective coefficients.

table.Description: Name of the density field (default is rho)
Name of the velocity field (default is U)

title: overpressure
content_summary: This function object calculates and writes the overpressure, which is the pressure relative to a specified reference pressure (typically ambient). The resulting overpressure field can also be saved for additional sampling.
content: This function object calculates and writes the overpressure (e.g. the pressure relative to a given reference pressure, usually ambient) given a pressure field and reference pressure. The field can also be saved to allow for additional sampling.

table.Description: Name of the pressure field (default is p)
Is the field saved (default is no)

title: impulse
content_summary: This function object calculates the impulse in each cell by integrating the overpressure over time, using a specified pressure field and reference pressure. The impulse field is automatically saved by default to allow for further sampling.
content: This function object calculates the impulse in every cell by integrating the overpressure over time using the specified pressure field and reference pressure. The impulse field is automatically saved to allow additional sampling by default.

table.Description: Name of the pressure field (default is p)

title: timeOfArrival
content_summary: This utility tracks the maximum pressure in each cell and records the time at which this maximum is reached.
content: This function object tracks the maximum pressure and records the time when the maximum pressure is reached.

table.Description: Name of the pressure field (default is p)

title: Utilities
content_summary: This chapter lists utilities developed for blastFoam to simplify the setup of cases and post-processing of results.
content: Listed below are the utilities that have been developed for blastFoam to simplify case setup and post-processing.

title: setRefinedFields
content_summary: This section describes 'setRefinedFields', a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM 'setFields' utility. It adds the capability to refine the mesh based on the fields being set, which is particularly useful for initializing small volumes within large computational cells. The utility allows for setting and refining 2D and 3D geometries, with refinement options specified in 'setFieldsDict'. It operates by setting default field values, applying the requested field modifications, and then checking for local variations to trigger refinement. The utility iterates until the mesh is stable or a maximum number of iterations is reached.
content:
This is a modified version of the setFields utility in the standard Open-FOAM that includes the ability to refine the mesh based on the fields that are set. This is extremely beneficial when a small volume needs to be set within a set of very large computational cells. The same functionality presented in Sec. 11 is present, allowing for 2D and 3D geometries to be set and refined, and the refinement options are specified in setFieldsDict, not dynamicMeshDict. The utility works by setting the default field values provided, setting the requested fields, then checking for local variations in specific fields. Region may have a level defined where the mesh is refined up to this level. The default field values for any fields being set must also be defined so that the region is updated at each iteration. The utility will iterate until the mesh is no longer changing, or the maximum number of iterations is reached. By default, no error estimator will be used so any local change in a tracked field will be refined. However, by specifying an error estimator, the refinement will be determined by the method requested.
By default only fields that are specified within setFieldsDict will be modified, meaning that all other fields must use uniform field values to be read. The ’-updateAll’ command line option will read in all fields present in the current time step and update the sizes of the fields which is necessary if non-uniform fields will be used. If balancing is used this will automatically be enabled to ensure that all fields are correct. In addition to the standard shapes that can be set with setFields, several additional options have been added and are listed below with their required entries. All *MassToCell options require ”rho” and ”mass” to be specified.
Usage: setRefinedFields [OPTIONS]
New cell sets:
- sphereListToCell: centres, radii, or radius
- cylinderCellToList: p1s, p2s, radii, or radius
- boxMassToCell: centre and aspectRatio (span for backup)
- cylindericalMassToCell: centre, LbyD and direction (radius and L for backup)
- sphericalMassToCell: centre (radius for backup)
- hemisphericalMassToCell: centre (radius for backup)
- searchableSurfaceToCell: surface (searchable surface type)
- searchableSurfaceToPoint: surface (searchable surface type)
The option to specify non-uniform values for the fields has also been included and are specified by using the field type (i.e. volScalarField, pointVectorField, etc.) followed by the type (i.e. Value, Density, etc.), for example pointScalarFieldValue, volVectorFieldDensity, etc.
Field set types:
- Density: A density is calculated using the given volume integrated quantity, and dividing by the volume of the selected cells
- Function: Values are set based on the specified function.
- InitialValue: The current field is read and saved to ensure the true values are used after refinement.
- MassIntegrated: The mass integrated quantity read and is divided by the mass (density times volume) of the given cell set to determine the field value.
- Sum: The field value is calculated given a summed value and the list of fields to use in the sum
- Value: A uniform value is set

title: blastToVTK
content_summary: This utility is designed to create smaller files for post-processing by converting data from a specified patch into VTK format. By default, it uses the same write interval as the main simulation, but an optional '-dt' flag allows for writing interpolated, intermediate time steps. A '.vtk.series' file is automatically generated for easy viewing in ParaView. The output is binary by default.
content:
In order to create smaller files for post-processing, blastToVTK can be used to create VTK files of a given patch with a set of fields. By Default the write interval will be the write interval of the VTK files, but optionally the ’-dt’ flag can be used to write interpolated, intermediate steps at the given interval. A *.vtk.series file will automatically be written for simplified viewing. By default the VTK files will be written in binary.
Usage: blastToVTK <patchName> [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Scheme used to interpolate between time steps (linearClamp by default)
List of fields to be written in the VTK files
Directory to write files to (VTK by default)

title: createTimeSeries
content_summary: This utility simplifies the creation of time series for visualization in ParaView. It generates a '*.series' file that contains the path to each time step of a VTK or STL file. This allows for easy viewing of the time evolution of the data. The utility creates a single series file for each sampled field in the provided directory. By default, it uses relative paths to the data files.
content:
This utility simplifies the previous utility by simply creating a *.*.series file which provides the path to each time step of a VTK or STL file and allows viewing of the time series with the time in ParaView. Only one additional file is created for each sampled field in the directory provided. The given path must include files within time step folders. By default the relative path to the files will be used
Usage: createTimeSeries <path/to/timeFolders> [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Directory to write the *.series file to

title: mergeProbes
content_summary: This is a simple utility for combining probe data files that were started at different times. By default, it merges all files found in the specified start time folder.
content:
This is a simple utility that can be used to calculate combine probes started at different times. By default, all files found in the start time folder will be merged.
Usage: mergeProbes <path/to/probes> [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Probed fields to merge (default is all)

title: calculateImpulse
content_summary: This is a simple utility for calculating impulse from a pressure probe file. It defaults to integrating the overpressure field if available, but this can be overridden with the '-p' option. If an overpressure file is used, the reference pressure is assumed to be 0 unless specified with the '-pRef' option.
content:
This is a simple utility that can be used to calculate the impulse using a pressure probe file. If an overpressure file is found, that will be the default field to integrate, but this can be overridden with the ’-p’ option. If an overpressure file is provided the reference pressure will be 0 by default, other with ’-pRef’ should be provided.
Usage: calculateImpulse <path/to/probes> [OPTIONS]

title: initializeAtmosphere
content_summary: The 'initializeAtmosphere' utility is used to set up stable initial pressure and density fields by balancing the hydrostatic pressure gradient with gravitational forces. It is a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM utility and offers two models: 'hydrostatic' and 'tabulated'. The hydrostatic model dynamically solves for the pressure gradient and adjusts the density, while the tabulated model uses a predefined list of pressures and temperatures versus height. An optional hydrostatic correction can be applied after using the tabulated data. Inputs can be provided via a dictionary or command line, and a 'system/atmosphereProperties' file is required for the tabulated option.
content:
The initializeAtmosphere utility is used to initialize stable pressure and density fields such that the hydrostatic pressure gradient and gravitational acceleration force are balanced. The hydrostatic initialization procedure is a modified version of that available in OpenFOAM (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018a). Currently, there are two available models: hydrostatic and tabulated. The hydrostatic model dynamically solves for the hydrostatic pressure gradient and adjusts the density accordingly. The tabulated model reads a list of pressures and temperatures versus height to determine the density fields. An optional hydrostatic correction can be used once the table values have been set. See tutorials/initializeAtmosphere for an example. Inputs can either be read from a dictionary or through the command line. If the tabulated option is requested, system/atmosphereProperies must be available.
Usage: initializeAtmosphere [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Reference height, or the height at the lowest point of the mesh [m]
Name of the phase to set (only for multiphase)
Optional cellZone to set (all cells by default)
Optional override of the model. (hydrostatic by default, no default if read from dictionary)

title: rotateFields
content_summary: The 'rotateFields' utility maps fields from a 1D or 2D solution to a 2D or 3D solution, using the source and target meshes to determine the necessary rotation. It correctly maps vector and tensor fields by applying the local rotation tensor. Only fields present in the target case are mapped by default, but additional fields can be included. For cells outside the mapped region, the closest value from the source mesh is used. The utility also supports iterative refinement based on criteria in 'dynamicMeshDict' to facilitate initialization with AMR. It is noted that the current mapping is not conservative and uses nearest-cell values, which can lead to over- or under-emphasis of flow features.
content:
The rotateFields utility is can be used to map the fields of a 1-D or 2-D solution to that of a 2-D or 3-D solution, where the source and target meshes are used to determine the rotation. The rotation of vector and tensor fields are mapped using the local rotation tensor to ensure that the correct radial components are used. Only field present in the target case are mapped, however additional fields can be mapped. Because there are no values for these fields, the closest value on the source mesh is used for cells outside of the mapped cells. Iterative refinement can be used to iteratively rotate fields and refine the mesh using the refinement criteria found in dynamicMeshDict allowing for easier initialization when using AMR.
NOTE: The mapping is not conservative currently so only the nearest cell value will be used which can result in certain flow features either being over or under emphasised.
Usage: rotateFields <sourceCase> [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Time in the source case to map (default is startFrom time in source case)
Region of the source case to map (only used for multi-region)
Region of the target case to map to (only used for multi-region)
If a cell is outside of the source range, map to the closest cell (default is no)
Cutoff radius to map (default is a big number)
Center of rotation on the source mesh (default is the center of the source mesh)
Copy uniform fields to the target case (default is no)
List of additional fields to map
Iteratively map, rotate and refine the mesh
Use cell tet decomposition (no by default)

title: convertLagrangianPositions
content_summary: This utility addresses an issue with ParaView's inability to read the barycentric coordinate format used by OpenFOAM for Lagrangian particle positions. It reads the standard OpenFOAM 'positions' file, converts it to a format readable by ParaView (for both serial and parallel cases), and rewrites the file. A '-revert' option is available to convert the positions back to the original format if needed to continue a simulation.
content:
Because OpenFOAM has changed the write format of Lagrangian particles to barycentric coordinated in place of a point in physical space, ParaView is no longer able to read the positions of particles any more. To alleviate this problem, and allow ParaView to read Lagrangian fields (in serial and parallel), the convertLagrangianPositions will read in the standard Open-FOAM positions file, convert it to a format that ParaView can read and rewrite the positions file. If the positions need to be reverted to continue a simulation, the ’-revert’ optional will rewrite the original positions of the particles.
Usage: convertLagrangianPositions [OPTIONS]

table.Description: Convert point positions back to original positions

title: reconstructParAll
content_summary: This is a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM 'reconstructPar' and 'reconstructParMesh' utilities. It is designed to handle the reconstruction of cases that have undergone dynamic load balancing, which would normally cause a fatal error with the standard utility. Mesh reconstruction is handled automatically, so 'reconstructParMesh' does not need to be run separately. This utility also supports the reconstruction of blastFoam AMR refinement histories, which is not typically supported by the standard tools. Notes are provided on using the '-constant' and '-withZero' flags when the mesh differs between directories.
content:
A modified version of the standard OpenFOAM reconstructPar and re-constructParMesh has been added to allow for reconstruction of cases that have been balanced, something that will cause a fatal error with reconstructPar. Reconstruction of the mesh is automaically handled, meaning that reconstructParMesh does not need to be run prior. This also allows for reconstruction of blastFoam AMR refinement histories which is not typically supported.
NOTE: If the mesh in the decomposed processor*/constant folder is different that that in the constant folder, the ’-constant’ must be used.
NOTE: If the mesh in the 0 folder differs from that in constant the ’-withZero’ flag must be used.

title: fvModels and fvConstraints
content_summary: This chapter explains that in addition to the built-in physics, additional source terms and models can be included in the simulation by using the optional 'constant/fvModels' and 'system/fvConstraints' directories. All such models must have their associated libraries included in their respective files. A list and brief description of these libraries are provided in Section 18.
content: In addition to the physics present in the standard solver, additional source terms and models can be included by adding the optional constant/fvModels and system/fvConstraints. All of these models must have their associated libraries included within the given entries in the files. A list of the libraries and a brief description can be found in Sec. 18

title: Optional libraries
content_summary: This chapter provides a list of optional libraries that can be included for functionObjects, fvModels, and fvConstraints. Including these libraries makes additional runtime-selectable models available.
content:
Below is a list of optional libraries to include for functionObject, fvModels, and fvConstraints. When libraries are included additional options will become available in the list of runtime-selectable models.
- "libutilityFunctionObjects.so": General utitilies
- "libfieldFunctionObjects.so": Function objects used for field quantities
- "liblagrangianFunctionObjects.so": Simple lagrangian particles
- "libforces.so": Calculate viscous and pressure forces activing on boundaries
- "libfvConstraints.so": General fvConstraints
- "libfvModels.so": General fvModels
- "liblagrangianParcel.so": Coupled lagrangian particles (one way coupling)

title: Solvers
content_summary: This chapter provides a brief description of the wide variety of solvers available in blastFoam for simulating transient, highly compressible flows.
content: blastFoam offers a wide variety of solvers for simulating transient, highly compressible flows. A short description of each is given below.

title: blastFoam
content_summary: The 'blastFoam' solver is the standard solver, capable of handling single-phase, two-phase, and multiphase flows. The phases and thermodynamic models are specified in the 'constant/phaseProperties' file. If a list with more than one phase is provided, the two-phase or multiphase solver is automatically selected; otherwise, the single-phase solver is used. All phases utilize the blastFoam-specific equations of state described in Section 5.
content: The blastFoam solver is the standard solver and has a variety of uses, including single phase, two phase and multiphase flows, where the phases and thermodynamic models are specified in the constant/phaseProperties. When a list of phases is provided (and the list has more than one entry), the two phase or multiphase solver is selected. If the key word phases is not specified, then the single phase solver is selected. All phases use the blastFoam specific equations of states (see Section 5).

title: blastEulerFoam
content_summary: The 'blastEulerFoam' solver is an Euler-Euler (two/multifluid) variant of blastFoam, based on the work of Houim and Oran (2016) and Lai et al. (2018). It currently supports fluid-solid simulations, with fluid-fluid capabilities under development. A key advantage over the standard 'multiphaseEulerFoam' is its ability to handle any number of particle phases. It supports three phase models: 'fluid' (for single-component fluids like air), 'multiphase' (for fluids with multiple components, each with a unique equation of state), and 'granular' (for solid phases). Fluid and multiphase models can use any of the fluid thermodynamic models, while the granular model uses solid thermodynamic models. Interactions between fluids are described in Sections 3 and 4. A note clarifies that while multiple fluid phases are technically supported, simulations of that type are not currently the intended use.
content:
The blastEulerFoam solver is the Euler-Euler (two/multifluid) variant of blastFoam. Currently only fluid-solid simulations are supported; however, the addition of fluid-fluid simulations is under development. This solver is based on the work of Houim and Oran (2016) and Lai et al. (2018). One major advantage over the standard multiphaseEulerFoam solver is that any number of particle phases can be used. Currently there are three phase models that can be used: fluid, multiphase, and granular. The fluid phase model is used to describe a single component fluid (such as air). The multiphase phase model is used to describe a fluid phase consisting of multiple phases, each described by a unique equation of state. This allows simulations of standard detonation problems with the addition of particles. The granular phase model is used to describe a solid phase. Both the fluid and multiphase models can use any of the fluid thermodynamic models, while the granular model can use any of the solid thermodynamic models. The models describing interactions between fluids are described in Sec. 3 and Sec. 4
NOTE: While multiple fluid phases will not cause the solver to fail, theses types of simulations are not currently supported.

title: blastFSIFoam
content_summary: The 'blastFSIFoam' solver handles fluid-solid interaction by using the standard 'blastFoam' solver for the fluid phase and models from Section 13 for the solid deformation. The fluid phase employs a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total Lagrangian, linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. Information transfer between patches is managed by the 'coupledGlobalPolyPatch' from Cardiff et al. (2018), which does not require changes to mesh patch types. Fields needing coupling information (like temperature or displacement) should use a global patch type (see Section 8.1). If meshes are created for each region individually, patch types can be set as usual; however, if the 'splitMeshRegions' utility is used, patch names and types may need modification. This solver is still in development.
content:
The blastFSIFoam solver uses the standard blastFoam solver to solve the fluid phase, and the models mentioned in Sec. 13 to handle the solid deformation. The fluid phase uses a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total lagrangian or linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. In order to correctly transfer the information between the patches, the coupledGlobalPolyPatch Cardiff et al. (2018) is used and does not require any changed to the mesh patch types. Fields that require coupling information (temperature, displacment, etc.) should used a global patch type (see Sec. 8.1). If meshes are made for each individual region, the patch types can be specified as usual. However, if the splitMeshRegions utility is used to separate a single region mesh into multiple region meshes, the patch names and types may need to be modified
NOTE: This solver is still in development. Please use with caution.

title: blastXiFoam
content_summary: This is an additional solver for combustion, extending the standard OpenFOAM 'XiFoam' solver. The key difference is the use of the flux schemes presented earlier to transport conserved quantities, which provides a more accurate description of highly compressible combustion phenomena like deflagration-to-detonation transition. It is recommended to use the 'adiabaticFlameT' utility to calculate model coefficients for the 'constant/thermophysicalProperties' dictionary.
content: In addition to the standard blastFoam solver, an additional solver has been added to solve combustion as an extension of the standard OpenFOAM Xi-Foam solver. The only difference is that the flux schemes presented earlier are used to transport the conserved quantities. This allows for a more accurate description of combusting, highly compressible flows (i.e. deflagration to detonation transition). It is recommended to use the adibaticFlameT utility to calculate the model coefficients used in the required constant/thermophysicalProperties dictionary.

title: blastReactingFoam
content_summary: The 'blastReactingFoam' solver uses standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes to solve multi-species systems with reactions, including combustion models. It requires the 'constant/thermophysicalProperties', 'constant/combustionProperties', and 'constant/chemistryProperties' dictionaries, along with initial mass fraction files. The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, energy) and mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes described in this guide.
content: blastReactingFoam uses the standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes, and can be used to solve multi-specie systems that include reactions. The use of combustion models are also available. The constant/thermophysicalProperties dictionary must be included under the constant dictionary along with the relevant dictionaries (constant/combustionProperties and constant/chemistyProperties) and initial value files (mass fractions). The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, and energy) as well as the mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes presented herein.

title: blastMultiRegionFoam
content_summary: The 'blastMultiRegionFoam' solver is derived from the 'chtMultiRegionFoam' solver in OpenFOAM. The main differences are the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. Thermal transport in solids is still handled implicitly using a matrix solution.
content: The blastMultiRegionFoam solver is derived from the chtMultiRegion-Foam available in OpenFOAM. The major differences are in the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. The thermal transport in solids is still treated implicitly through a matrix solution.

title: blastParcelFoam
content_summary: The 'blastParcelFoam' solver uses standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian classes to simulate a coupled system of a Eulerian fluid and a collection of Lagrangian parcels. It includes interaction terms for drag, heat transfer, and particle collisions. Not all standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian options are available due to the use of a different thermodynamic model. While AMR can be used with this solver, dynamic load balancing cannot.
content: blastParcelFoam makes use of the standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian classes to solve a coupled Eulerian fluid and collection of Lagragian parcels. This includes interaction terms like drag, heat transfer, and particle collisions. Not all of the OpenFOAM standard Lagrangian options are available due to the use of a different thermodynamic model. While AMR can be used, dynamic load balancing cannot.

title: blastEquation
content_summary: The 'blastEquation' utility leverages the numerics library to evaluate simple or complex coded function evaluations. It can be used either through the command line or with dictionaries to define equations and operations. Using dictionaries provides more control, especially for root-solving, minimization, and integration schemes. The tutorials for this utility offer more complete examples.
content: blastEquation makes use of the numerics library and can be used to interrogate simple equations, or more complicated, coded function evaluations. This utility can either be used strictly though the command line, or using dictionaries to define equations and the operations they are used with. More control is available when using dictionaries, specifically with respect to root solving, minimization, and integration schemes. Please see tutorials/blastEquation for more complete examples on how to use this utility.
